title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Part A explained how trials work
The rules that protect participants were written after real abuses
This part puts those rules to work on hard, current cases
The hard question: when does reviewed, legal, well-funded research still wrong people?





By the end of Part B, you will be able to:

Apply the Belmont principles as concrete IRB review questions
Identify vulnerable populations and the protections they require
Judge when placebo controls are and are not ethically defensible
Analyze the short-course AZT trials as a justice debate
Explain why research on tissue and data (HeLa) needs its own consent rules
Describe recent developments: the rewritten Common Rule, trial-diversity law, and research misconduct

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
2.1 A Quick Three-Case Scoreboard








Case
Main wrong highlighted
Protection it pushed into focus




Nazi experiments
No real consent; extreme harm
Voluntary consent; Nuremberg (Nuremberg Military Tribunals 1947)


Willowbrook
Dependent children used in a coercive setting
Extra protection for vulnerable populations; independent review (Jonsen 1998)


Tuskegee

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 2.2 Tuskegee: A Study That Kept Going
text:
2.2 Tuskegee: A Study That Kept Going



Began in 1932 under the U.S. Public Health Service (Brandt 1978)
Poor Black men in Alabama were told they had “bad blood,” not syphilis
Even after penicillin became effective, treatment was withheld
The study lasted for 40 years
Tuskegee is not just about bad science; it is about racism, deception, and institutional endurance




Public domain image from CDC archives, via Wikimedia Commons.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 2.3 Willowbrook: The Only Bed with a Catch
text:
2.3 Willowbrook: The Only Bed with a Catch



Children with intellectual disabilities were admitted to an overcrowded institution
Researchers deliberately infected some children with hepatitis to study its course and possible prevention
Parents were often asked to consent in conditions of desperation
The defense was that many children would have been exposed anyway
Willowbrook turned “consent” into a test of whether a constrained choice is a real choice at all (Jonsen 1998)




Willowbrook State School postcard, public domain, via Wikimedia Commons and the New York Public Library.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 2.4 Nazi Experiments: The Point of Nuremberg
text:
2.4 Nazi Experiments: The Point of Nuremberg

In Nazi camps, prisoners were used as research material under coercion and terror (Nuremberg Military Tribunals 1947)
There was no meaningful refusal
The Nuremberg Code answered with its clearest rule: voluntary consent is essential
That rule sounds obvious now because the abuse was so extreme
But later scandals showed that ordinary institutions also needed rules, not just monsters

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
2.2 Thought Question

What was the deepest wrong shared by these cases?

People were used as means to an end
People could not refuse freely
Powerful institutions targeted the vulnerable
Researchers treated knowledge as more important than persons


Which answer seems most central to you, and why?
Are these really different wrongs, or just different ways of naming the same one?
If you had to design one protection first, which wrong would you aim it at?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
2.3 Make It Contemporary

Today the abuse is less likely to look like camps or segregated wards.

It may look like a confusing consent form.
It may look like recruiting the people least able to say no.
It may look like calling exploitation “access.”

If a study is legal, reviewed, and well funded, what warning signs would still make you uneasy?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
2.4 After You’ve Discussed






Note



Three distinctions are worth keeping sharp:

Consent failures, harm failures, and justice failures are not identical. Tuskegee had all three.
These cases are not only about cruelty. They are also about institutions finding reassuring language for doing the wrong thing.
Modern oversight exists because good intentions are not enough. The whole point is to distrust self-justification before the damage is done.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.1 Three Belmont Principles

The Belmont Report distilled U.S. research ethics into three principles in 1979 (National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research 1979)
Respect for persons
Beneficence
Justice
These are not abstract decorations; they are meant to govern real study design

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.2 Respect for Persons

Treat participants as agents, not material
Make informed consent real, not ceremonial
Protect those with reduced autonomy through additional safeguards
Allow refusal and withdrawal without punishment
Ask first whether the person understands what kind of activity this is

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.3 Beneficence

Minimize likely harms
Make sure the research question is worth asking
Compare risks against likely benefits to participants and society
Stop or change a study if harms become unjustified (World Medical Association 2024)
Beneficence is about disciplined judgment, not vague goodwill

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.4 Justice

Ask who bears the burdens of research
Ask who receives the benefits of the knowledge produced
Watch for convenient recruitment of dependent or stigmatized groups
Also watch for wrongful exclusion from studies that may help shape future care (World Medical Association 2024)
Justice is about selection, not only outcomes

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 3.6 What an IRB Tries to Do
text:
3.6 What an IRB Tries to Do

An IRB asks whether the study question matters enough to justify involving people at all (U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Office for Human Research Protections 2026)
It asks whether risks are minimized
It asks whether selection is fair
It asks whether the consent process is understandable and voluntary
It exists because the research team should not be the only judge of its own ethics

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
4.1 Who Counts as Vulnerable?

Some groups face special pressure, dependence, or limited freedom in research settings
In U.S. regulation, children, prisoners, and pregnant people receive additional protections under specific subparts of 45 CFR 46 (U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Office for Human Research Protections 2026)
Cognitive impairment, economic hardship, and dependent relationships can also create vulnerability (World Medical Association 2024)
Vulnerability is not a personality type
It is usually about power, context, and what saying no would cost
Justice cuts both ways: wrongful exclusion from research is also a failure

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
4.2 Vulnerable Populations and Typical Protections








Group
Main worry
Typical protection




Children
Limited legal and developmental capacity
Parent/guardian permission plus child assent when possible


Prisoners
Constrained freedom and risk of coercion
Narrower allowable research categories and extra scrutiny


Pregnant people / fetuses
Risk to more than one party and historical exclusion
Additional risk justification and review


People with cognitive impairment
Difficulty understanding or sustaining choice
Capacity assessment, surrogate consent, respect for assent or dissent


Economically disadvantaged participants
Undue inducement and convenience recruitment
Careful payment review and fair selection

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 4.3 Inclusion Can Also Be a Justice Issue
text:
4.3 Inclusion Can Also Be a Justice Issue

Ethics is not only about over-inclusion
Women, minority groups, older adults, and pregnant people have often been excluded from research
Then treatments are approved with weaker evidence for the people who will actually use them
So justice has two dangers: dumping risk on the vulnerable and withholding the benefits of evidence from them (World Medical Association 2024)
Fair selection means avoiding both

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
4.4 Thought Question

You are on an IRB reviewing a vaccine study.

The disease burden is high in prisons.
Researchers want to recruit incarcerated people because follow-up will be easy.
They plan a modest payment and better clinic access for participants.


Is this fair inclusion, opportunistic targeting, or both?
What extra protections would you demand before approving it?
Would excluding prisoners entirely also create a justice problem?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
4.5 Make It Contemporary

A company launches a drug trial but excludes pregnant people, many disabled adults, and people with low literacy because follow-up will be “messier.”

The drug will still be marketed broadly if approved.

Is this caution, convenience, or injustice?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
3.4 After You’ve Discussed






Note




Respect for persons: surrogate consent is allowed, but the residents’ own assent — and dissent — still count. A child’s signature is not the whole story.
Beneficence: a 12-month placebo arm in a progressive disease needs serious justification (U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Office for Human Research Protections 2026).
Justice: “they don’t move” is convenience. Convenience is exactly what fair selection forbids.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
6.1 Helsinki’s Placebo Rule

The modern Declaration of Helsinki says new interventions should usually be tested against the best proven intervention (World Medical Association 2024)
Placebo is acceptable if no proven intervention exists
It may also be acceptable for compelling methodological reasons
But not if participants face additional risk of serious or irreversible harm by going without the proven treatment (World Medical Association 2024)
That last condition is the one people fight about

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
6.2 Why This Rule Is Hard

Sometimes the best global treatment is unavailable in the place where the study is being done
A shorter, cheaper, or simpler regimen might still save many lives
Researchers then ask whether the ethically relevant comparison is the best global care or the best locally available care
That is not a purely technical question
It is a question about whether poverty changes what researchers may permissibly offer

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
section: 6.3 The AZT Trials in Five Lines
text:
6.3 The AZT Trials in Five Lines

In 1994, a long-course zidovudine regimen reduced perinatal HIV transmission
That regimen was expensive and logistically difficult in many low-resource settings
Later trials tested shorter, cheaper regimens, often against placebo
Critics argued this withheld a known effective intervention (Lurie and Wolfe 1997)
Defenders argued the real question was what feasible strategy could work where the long regimen was not realistically available (Varmus and Satcher 1997)

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
6.4 Two Famous Positions


Lurie and Wolfe (Lurie and Wolfe 1997)

A proven regimen already existed
Placebo meant withholding known benefit
Poverty should not lower the ethical standard
Participants in poorer countries should not get weaker protections


Varmus and Satcher (Varmus and Satcher 1997)

The long regimen was not realistically implementable in many settings
A shorter regimen might still save lives at scale
The ethically relevant comparison had to include feasibility
Rejecting the trials might preserve principle while blocking practical benefit

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
6.7 Thought Question

You are on an ethics committee in 1997 reviewing a short-course AZT trial in Uganda.

In the sponsor country, the long-course regimen is already known to work.
In the host country, that regimen is too expensive and too complex for ordinary use.
The proposed study compares a shorter regimen to placebo.


What is the right comparison group?
Does local unavailability change what researchers ethically owe participants?
If the shorter regimen could save many more babies in practice, does that justify the placebo arm?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
5.2 Make It Contemporary

You give a blood sample for a routine test. A leftover vial is stored.

Years later it is used — deidentified — in genetic research and a profitable diagnostic.
You were never specifically asked. A broad form at intake said “samples may be used for research.”


Was that consent meaningful?
Do you have a claim to a say — or a share — in what was discovered?
Is “deidentified” enough, when the sample is DNA?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
4.6 After You’ve Discussed






Note



Three points to keep in play:

Vulnerability is about power, not just fragility. Ask who depends on whom, who can walk away, and who cannot.
Extra protections should not become automatic exclusion. Protecting people is not the same as making them invisible.
Convenience is the enemy. Easy to recruit is never enough reason by itself.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
8.1 The Point of Protection

Research needs volunteers
Volunteers are not raw material
That is why consent, independent review, fair selection, and post-trial duties belong together
The rulebook exists to keep learning answerable to persons
When it works, research becomes both scientifically stronger and morally clearer

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
8.2 Wrapping Up Part B

Research-ethics rules are only as good as their use: Belmont as live IRB questions, real protections for vulnerable groups, hard limits on placebo when effective treatment exists, the AZT debate over justice across borders, the tissue-and-data problems Henrietta Lacks still poses, and today’s evolving rules — the rewritten Common Rule, diversity requirements, and the fight against research misconduct. The central lesson is demanding: ethical research is not research with a signature, but research designed — during and after — so that the people who make knowledge possible are treated as persons first.

text:

Does local unavailability change what researchers ethically owe participants?
If the shorter regimen could save many more babies in practice, does that justify the placebo arm?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Make It Contemporary

You give a blood sample for a routine test. A leftover vial is stored.

Years later it is used — deidentified — in genetic research and a profitable diagnostic.
You were never specifically asked. A broad form at intake said “samples may be used for research.”


Was that consent meaningful?
Do you have a claim to a say — or a share — in what was discovered?
Is “deidentified” enough, when the sample is DNA?

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


Three points to keep in play:

Vulnerability is about power, not just fragility. Ask who depends on whom, who can walk away, and who cannot.
Extra protections should not become automatic exclusion. Protecting people is not the same as making them invisible.
Convenience is the enemy. Easy to recruit is never enough reason by itself.

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
The Point of Protection

Research needs volunteers
Volunteers are not raw material
That is why consent, independent review, fair selection, and post-trial duties belong together
The rulebook exists to keep learning answerable to persons
When it works, research becomes both scientifically stronger and morally clearer

title: Lecture 3 — Research Ethics
text:
Wrapping Up Part B

Research-ethics rules are only as good as their use: Belmont as live IRB questions, real protections for vulnerable groups, hard limits on placebo when effective treatment exists, the AZT debate over justice across borders, the tissue-and-data problems Henrietta Lacks still poses, and today’s evolving rules — the rewritten Common Rule, diversity requirements, and the fight against research misconduct. The central lesson is demanding: ethical research is not research with a signature, but research designed — during and after — so that the people who make knowledge possible are treated as persons first.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Part A showed why unchecked professional authority is dangerous
But clinicians still have expertise, duties, and time pressure
Patients still come to medicine for guidance, not just facts
So what should replace “doctor knows best”? That is Part B.





By the end of Part B, you will be able to:

Distinguish the four Emanuel and Emanuel models
Explain the four elements of valid informed consent
Tell an unwise decision apart from an incapacitated one
Identify the main limits on confidentiality
Work through a truth-telling conflict involving family and culture

So what should replace “doctor knows best”? That is Part B.

text:

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
2.1 Meet Mrs. R

Mrs. R is 61. She came in for fatigue and weight loss.
The colonoscopy found colon cancer — serious, but treatable.
She is frightened. Her daughter, Dana, drives her to every appointment and does most of the talking.
We will follow Mrs. R through the whole class. Every idea today will land on her.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
2.2 What’s Missing From This Picture?




Sir Luke Fildes, The Doctor (1891), via Wikimedia Commons, public domain.



The iconic image of medicine: the doctor watches, interprets, decides
The patient is silent
The family waits in the background
One thing is missing that we now think is central: the patient’s voice

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: 2.3 Why the Clinical Relationship Is Hard
text:
2.3 Why the Clinical Relationship Is Hard

Patients are sick, frightened, and often in pain
Information is unequal from the very first minute
Values are at stake, not just technical facts
Patients often want guidance — but guidance can quietly become control

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
2.4 Three Sources of Asymmetry







asym


P

Patient
ill, vulnerable,
time-pressured



C

Clinician
expertise, options,
interpretation



C->P






I

Institution
policy, cost,
workflow



I->P






F

Family / others
expectations, pressure,
care roles



F->P







 A compact map of the clinical asymmetry: the patient sits at the center, but expertise, institutions, and family pressures all shape the decision space. 




Ethical care does not erase this asymmetry — it manages it openly

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
2.5 The Core Tension

Autonomy versus Beneficence


Autonomy: the patient’s right to make informed choices
Beneficence: the clinician’s duty to protect welfare
Most of the time these agree
The hard cases are when they pull apart — and Mrs. R is about to give us one

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
3.1 Four Models at a Glance








Model
Main clinician role
Main risk




Paternalistic
Decide what is best
Patient becomes passive


Informative
Provide facts and options
Clinician becomes a vending machine


Interpretive
Help clarify the patient’s values
Clinician may over-read the patient


Deliberative
Recommend and reason about values
Persuasion can slide into pressure

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
3.2 One Map of the Models







models


A

How should a clinician
use authority?



B

Patient values
already clear?



A->B





C

If yes, what should
the clinician do?

yes

the end

C->I

R

patient's values

clarify values



D

recommend and
give reasons



C->D


recommend values




 The Emanuel models differ mainly in what the clinician does with professional authority once a patient’s values are more or less clear.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
3.3 The Same News, Four Ways (1)
Mrs. R asks: “So what happens now?” Same clinician, same facts, four models:

Paternalistic — “The right move is surgery. We’ll get you scheduled.”

Risk: Mrs. R becomes a passenger in her own care

Informative — “Here are three options with their survival numbers. Which do you want?”

Risk: the facts get dumped; she’s left to interpret them alone

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
3.4 The Same News, Four Ways (2)

Interpretive — “Before we choose: what matters most to you — more time, or fewer side effects?”

Risk: the clinician may over-read what she “really” wants

Deliberative — “Here’s what I’d weigh in your situation, and why. What do you think?”

Risk: good persuasion can tip into pressure



Read these aloud in four different voices. The point students should feel: the facts never changed — only what the clinician did with authority.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
3.5 Which Model Fits?

Match a model to each case — and say why:

An unconscious trauma patient in the ED
A patient choosing between two cancer regimens with similar odds
An older adult unsure whether dialysis still fits her goals
A patient who asks you, “What would you do if it were your mom?”

And: should Mrs. R get the same model at every visit?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
3.6 Make It Contemporary

Mrs. R arrives at the next visit having read everything online. She is convinced she needs a specific drug she saw in an ad, and an AI chatbot agreed with her.

Does the “informative” model still work when a patient can’t judge their own information?
Is telling her the source is wrong paternalistic, or just honest?
What does respecting her autonomy actually require here?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
3.7 After You’ve Discussed






Note




No model wins everywhere. Emergencies push left toward paternalism; chronic illness usually works best in the interpretive/deliberative middle.
Choosing the model is itself a shared decision. The deepest move is to ask the patient how much steering they want — and to revisit it as the illness, and the patient, change. “Advice” with the steering hidden is the move to watch for.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: 4.1 Consent Is a Process, Not a Signature
text:
4.1 Consent Is a Process, Not a Signature

Informed consent makes “respect for persons” concrete (Faden and Beauchamp 1986)
A signed form can document the process — it cannot replace it
A refusal can be just as valid as a yes
Outside emergencies, consent comes before the intervention

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: 4.2 The Four Elements of Valid Consent
text:
4.2 The Four Elements of Valid Consent



Element
The practical question




Disclosure
Was the relevant information provided?


Understanding
Does the patient actually grasp it?


Voluntariness
Is the choice free from controlling pressure?


Capacity
Can the patient make this decision now?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
4.3 Disclosure & Understanding, with Mrs. R

Disclosure — you explain the cancer, the surgery, the major risks, the alternatives, and what happens if she does nothing
Understanding — you ask her to say it back: “What did you hear?”

She says: “You’re removing part of my colon, and I might need a bag for a while.”
That is understanding. Hearing the words is not the same thing.

Plain language, an interpreter when needed, and slow down when fear or pain narrows her focus

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
4.4 Voluntariness, with Mrs. R

Dana answers every question before her mother can
Asked what she wants, Mrs. R says quietly: “Whatever my daughter thinks is best.”
Is that a free choice — or pressure dressed up as family love?
A formally signed choice can still be a non-voluntary one

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: 4.5 Capacity Is Not What People Think
text:
4.5 Capacity Is Not What People Think

Capacity is decision-specific (Appelbaum 2007)
Capacity is time-specific
Capacity is not the same as intelligence
Capacity is not the same as having a mental illness
Capacity is not the same as agreeing with the clinician

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
4.6 A Practical Capacity Check

Ask whether the patient can (Appelbaum 2007):

communicate a stable choice
understand the relevant information
appreciate how it applies to them
reason about the options coherently

 A slide-sized informed-consent checklist. Each gate asks a different question, so a signed form alone is never enough.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
4.8 Refusal Is the Real Test

Easy cases are usually consent cases
The hard cases are refusals: the Jehovah’s Witness patient, leaving against medical advice
The course’s hardest refusal case is Dax Cowart

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
4.9 Dax Cowart

Dax Cowart suffered catastrophic burns in 1973
He repeatedly asked that treatment stop
Clinicians kept treating him, for years, against his stated wishes
He later argued that his autonomy had been denied (Cowart and Burt 1998)
The clearest collision of autonomy and beneficence in the course


This is the paired case study. Students don’t need the full facts now — only enough to feel the autonomy/beneficence collision before they read it.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
4.10 Thought Question

A competent adult refuses a blood transfusion that would almost certainly save their life.

Is honoring the refusal respect — or abandonment?
What is the clinician still obligated to keep doing after the refusal?
Mrs. R, in tears, says “I can’t even think about this right now.” Is that a refusal, indecision, or lost capacity — and how would you tell?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
4.11 Make It Contemporary

Change one fact: the refusal isn’t religious. The patient read online that transfusions are dangerous and won’t budge.

Same legal right. Does the reason change what you owe them?
Does correcting the misinformation respect her autonomy — or undermine it?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
4.12 After You’ve Discussed






Note




Respecting a decision is not endorsing the reasoning. You can believe she is wrong and still be bound by her refusal.
The capacity bar is applied unevenly. We rarely test capacity when patients say yes; we scrutinize it when they say no. If every risky refusal counted as incapacity, informed consent collapses back into paternalism. The honest question is what danger is enough to re-assess — not to override.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
5.1 Why Confidentiality Matters

Patients tell the truth because they expect privacy
Confidentiality is ethical before it is legal
It protects dignity, not just data
Without it, clinical honesty breaks down

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: 5.2 HIPAA Is Not a Gag Order
text:
5.2 HIPAA Is Not a Gag Order

HIPAA is the federal health-privacy rule (U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Office for Civil Rights 2025)
It is not a vow of total silence
It permits sharing for treatment, payment, and operations — and to warn of serious danger
Concretely: if Mrs. R’s sister calls for an update, without Mrs. R’s OK you generally can’t even confirm she’s a patient
The ethical question is when disclosure is justified, not merely legal

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
5.3 HHS on the Privacy Rule


“A major goal of the Privacy Rule is to assure that individuals’ health information is properly protected while allowing the flow of health information needed to provide and promote high quality health care and to protect the public’s health and well being. The Rule strikes a balance that permits important uses of information, while protecting the privacy of people who seek care and healing.”


U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Summary of the HIPAA Privacy Rule (U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Office for Civil Rights 2025)

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
5.4 May Disclose vs. Must Disclose


You may disclose when

The patient authorizes it
It is necessary for treatment
Law permits limited operational use
A serious threat may justify a warning
Legal process requires a response


You must disclose

Suspected child abuse or neglect
Certain infectious diseases
Some court-ordered disclosures
Some state-mandated reporting
(The exact list varies by state)

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
5.5 Confidentiality: Default and Exceptions







conf


A

Protected health
information



B

Default:
keep private



A->B





C

Patient authorizes
sharing



B->C


exception



D

Law requires
reporting



B->D


exception



E

Serious threat
may justify warning



B->E


exception




 Confidentiality starts from privacy, not disclosure. The legal and ethical question is which exceptions are strong enough to override that default.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
5.6 Tarasoff in Five Beats

In Tarasoff, Prosenjit Poddar told a therapist he planned to kill Tatiana Tarasoff (Supreme Court of California 1976)
Police briefly detained him and then released him
Tarasoff was not effectively warned
Poddar later killed her
The case became the classic limit on absolute confidentiality

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
5.7 In the Court’s Words


“We conclude that the public policy favoring protection of the confidential character of patient-psychotherapist communications must yield to the extent to which disclosure is essential to avert danger to others. The protective privilege ends where the public peril begins.”


Tarasoff v. Regents of the University of California (1976) (Supreme Court of California 1976)

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: 5.8 The Case Nobody Warns You About
text:
5.8 The Case Nobody Warns You About

You are a nursing student on a med-surg unit. Your old roommate is admitted two doors down — and you have chart access.
You’re genuinely worried about her. You open her chart “just to see if she’s okay.” You tell no one. No one is harmed.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
5.9 Thought Question

Start with the roommate’s chart, then turn the privacy default on its head:

You looked “just to check she’s okay.” What exactly is the wrong, if no one ever finds out?
Does it change if she were your patient? If she’d want you to know? If you tell no one?
Now a patient tells you: “Sometimes I think about killing my ex — I know where she works.” What would you need to know before warning anyone?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
5.10 Make It Contemporary


Hospitals run automated access audits; every year nurses are fired for snooping on relatives, exes, and celebrities
A patient posts a specific threat on Instagram, not in your office

Does “do I need this to care for this patient?” still draw the line in the digital version — both for the snooping and for the threat?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
5.11 After You’ve Discussed






Note




Access is not authorization. Being able to open a chart is not permission. The test is “do I need this to care for this patient?” Curiosity, worry, and good intentions are not exceptions.
The default flips, but the question doesn’t. Snooping over-discloses; Tarasoff under-discloses. Both are answered the same way — by asking what is genuinely necessary: to provide care, or to prevent serious, specific harm.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: 5.12 Back to Mrs. R: “Please Don’t Tell Her”
text:
5.12 Back to Mrs. R: “Please Don’t Tell Her”

The biopsy is worse than expected. Before you go in, Dana stops you in the hallway:

“In our family, we don’t tell elders news like this. It will crush her. Please talk to me, not her.”

The team wants to respect the family’s culture. The team also wants to respect Mrs. R.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
5.13 Thought Question


Whose decision is this — Mrs. R’s, Dana’s, or the team’s?
What is the first thing you should find out, and who do you ask?
What if you ask Mrs. R and she says, “I don’t want the details — talk to Dana”?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
5.14 Make It Contemporary

A genetic test shows Mrs. R carries a mutation her adult siblings may also carry.

She does not want them told. They may be making medical decisions without information that could protect them.
Does her privacy outweigh their need to know? Is this the same problem as the family request, or a different one?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
5.15 After You’ve Discussed






Note




Only Mrs. R can waive her own right to know. A competent patient may autonomously choose not to receive information (“just talk to my daughter”) — that is respected autonomy. The family withholding it from her, unasked, is not their call.
So it isn’t “autonomy vs. culture.” The move is to ask Mrs. R — before the bad news — how much she wants to know and whom she wants involved. Culturally informed, and still hers.
The genetic case is different in kind: a third party’s welfare is now at stake. That is closer to Tarasoff than to truth-telling.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
6.1 Mrs. R, Start to Finish

You met her as a frightened patient with a daughter who spoke for her
You chose a model with her, not for her
You made consent a conversation — and took her tears seriously without calling them incapacity
You kept her information private by default — and asked her, not Dana, how she wanted the truth handled

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
6.2 Two Mistakes to Avoid

Reducing autonomy to “the customer picks from a menu”
Treating every bad or emotional decision as proof of incapacity
Treating HIPAA as a gag order
Assuming one cultural script fits every patient

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
6.3 Paired Case: Dax Cowart

Read for next class: Case 1 — Dax Cowart


Severe burns and unbearable pain
Repeated refusal of treatment
Family, physicians, and patient pulling in different directions
One of the clearest tests of autonomy in clinical ethics

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
6.3 Wrapping Up Part B

Part A explained why modern medicine rejects unchecked paternalism. Part B asked what replaces it: guided, transparent, autonomy-respecting care. Following Mrs. R, we saw the four clinician-patient models, the four elements of valid consent, the difference between an unwise choice and an incapacitated one, and confidentiality and truth-telling as defaults with disciplined exceptions.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
6.4 References


Appelbaum, Paul S. 2007. “Assessment of Patients’ Competence to Consent to Treatment.” New England Journal of Medicine 357 (18): 1834–40. https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMcp074045.


Cowart, Dax, and Robert Burt. 1998. “Confronting Death: Who Chooses, Who Controls?” Hastings Center Report 28 (1): 14–24. https://doi.org/10.2307/3528337.

Mrs. R arrives at the next visit having read everything online. She is convinced she needs a specific drug she saw in an ad, and an AI chatbot agreed with her.

Does the “informative” model still work when a patient can’t judge their own information?
Is telling her the source is wrong paternalistic, or just honest?
What does respecting her autonomy actually require here?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note



No model wins everywhere. Emergencies push left toward paternalism; chronic illness usually works best in the interpretive/deliberative middle.
Choosing the model is itself a shared decision. The deepest move is to ask the patient how much steering they want — and to revisit it as the illness, and the patient, change. “Advice” with the steering hidden is the move to watch for.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: Consent Is a Process, Not a Signature
text:
Consent Is a Process, Not a Signature

Informed consent makes “respect for persons” concrete (Faden and Beauchamp 1986)
A signed form can document the process — it cannot replace it
A refusal can be just as valid as a yes
Outside emergencies, consent comes before the intervention

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: The Four Elements of Valid Consent
text:
The Four Elements of Valid Consent



Element
The practical question




Disclosure
Was the relevant information provided?


Understanding
Does the patient actually grasp it?


Voluntariness
Is the choice free from controlling pressure?


Capacity
Can the patient make this decision now?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Disclosure & Understanding, with Mrs. R

Disclosure — you explain the cancer, the surgery, the major risks, the alternatives, and what happens if she does nothing
Understanding — you ask her to say it back: “What did you hear?”

She says: “You’re removing part of my colon, and I might need a bag for a while.”
That is understanding. Hearing the words is not the same thing.

Plain language, an interpreter when needed, and slow down when fear or pain narrows her focus

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Voluntariness, with Mrs. R

Dana answers every question before her mother can
Asked what she wants, Mrs. R says quietly: “Whatever my daughter thinks is best.”
Is that a free choice — or pressure dressed up as family love?
A formally signed choice can still be a non-voluntary one

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: Capacity Is Not What People Think
text:
Capacity Is Not What People Think

Capacity is decision-specific (Appelbaum 2007)
Capacity is time-specific
Capacity is not the same as intelligence
Capacity is not the same as having a mental illness
Capacity is not the same as agreeing with the clinician

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
A Practical Capacity Check

Ask whether the patient can (Appelbaum 2007):

communicate a stable choice
understand the relevant information
appreciate how it applies to them
reason about the options coherently

text:
The Consent Checklist







consent

 A slide-sized informed-consent checklist. Each gate asks a different question, so a signed form alone is never enough.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Refusal Is the Real Test

Easy cases are usually consent cases
The hard cases are refusals: the Jehovah’s Witness patient, leaving against medical advice
The course’s hardest refusal case is Dax Cowart

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Dax Cowart

Dax Cowart suffered catastrophic burns in 1973
He repeatedly asked that treatment stop
Clinicians kept treating him, for years, against his stated wishes
He later argued that his autonomy had been denied (Cowart and Burt 1998)
The clearest collision of autonomy and beneficence in the course


This is the paired case study. Students don’t need the full facts now — only enough to feel the autonomy/beneficence collision before they read it.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Thought Question

A competent adult refuses a blood transfusion that would almost certainly save their life.

Is honoring the refusal respect — or abandonment?
What is the clinician still obligated to keep doing after the refusal?
Mrs. R, in tears, says “I can’t even think about this right now.” Is that a refusal, indecision, or lost capacity — and how would you tell?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Make It Contemporary

Change one fact: the refusal isn’t religious. The patient read online that transfusions are dangerous and won’t budge.

Same legal right. Does the reason change what you owe them?
Does correcting the misinformation respect her autonomy — or undermine it?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note



Respecting a decision is not endorsing the reasoning. You can believe she is wrong and still be bound by her refusal.
The capacity bar is applied unevenly. We rarely test capacity when patients say yes; we scrutinize it when they say no. If every risky refusal counted as incapacity, informed consent collapses back into paternalism. The honest question is what danger is enough to re-assess — not to override.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Why Confidentiality Matters

Patients tell the truth because they expect privacy
Confidentiality is ethical before it is legal
It protects dignity, not just data
Without it, clinical honesty breaks down

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: HIPAA Is Not a Gag Order
text:
HIPAA Is Not a Gag Order

HIPAA is the federal health-privacy rule (U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Office for Civil Rights 2025)
It is not a vow of total silence
It permits sharing for treatment, payment, and operations — and to warn of serious danger
Concretely: if Mrs. R’s sister calls for an update, without Mrs. R’s OK you generally can’t even confirm she’s a patient
The ethical question is when disclosure is justified, not merely legal

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
HHS on the Privacy Rule


“A major goal of the Privacy Rule is to assure that individuals’ health information is properly protected while allowing the flow of health information needed to provide and promote high quality health care and to protect the public’s health and well being. The Rule strikes a balance that permits important uses of information, while protecting the privacy of people who seek care and healing.”


U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Summary of the HIPAA Privacy Rule (U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Office for Civil Rights 2025)

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
May Disclose vs. Must Disclose


You may disclose when

The patient authorizes it
It is necessary for treatment
Law permits limited operational use
A serious threat may justify a warning
Legal process requires a response


You must disclose

Suspected child abuse or neglect
Certain infectious diseases
Some court-ordered disclosures
Some state-mandated reporting
(The exact list varies by state)

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Confidentiality: Default and Exceptions







conf


A

Protected health
information



B

Default:
keep private



A->B





C

Patient authorizes
sharing



B->C


exception



D

Law requires
reporting



B->D


exception



E

Serious threat
may justify warning



B->E


exception




 Confidentiality starts from privacy, not disclosure. The legal and ethical question is which exceptions are strong enough to override that default.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Tarasoff in Five Beats

In Tarasoff, Prosenjit Poddar told a therapist he planned to kill Tatiana Tarasoff (Supreme Court of California 1976)
Police briefly detained him and then released him
Tarasoff was not effectively warned
Poddar later killed her
The case became the classic limit on absolute confidentiality

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
In the Court’s Words


“We conclude that the public policy favoring protection of the confidential character of patient-psychotherapist communications must yield to the extent to which disclosure is essential to avert danger to others. The protective privilege ends where the public peril begins.”


Tarasoff v. Regents of the University of California (1976) (Supreme Court of California 1976)

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: The Case Nobody Warns You About
text:
The Case Nobody Warns You About

You are a nursing student on a med-surg unit. Your old roommate is admitted two doors down — and you have chart access.
You’re genuinely worried about her. You open her chart “just to see if she’s okay.” You tell no one. No one is harmed.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Thought Question

Start with the roommate’s chart, then turn the privacy default on its head:

You looked “just to check she’s okay.” What exactly is the wrong, if no one ever finds out?
Does it change if she were your patient? If she’d want you to know? If you tell no one?
Now a patient tells you: “Sometimes I think about killing my ex — I know where she works.” What would you need to know before warning anyone?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Make It Contemporary


Hospitals run automated access audits; every year nurses are fired for snooping on relatives, exes, and celebrities
A patient posts a specific threat on Instagram, not in your office

Does “do I need this to care for this patient?” still draw the line in the digital version — both for the snooping and for the threat?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note



Access is not authorization. Being able to open a chart is not permission. The test is “do I need this to care for this patient?” Curiosity, worry, and good intentions are not exceptions.
The default flips, but the question doesn’t. Snooping over-discloses; Tarasoff under-discloses. Both are answered the same way — by asking what is genuinely necessary: to provide care, or to prevent serious, specific harm.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: Back to Mrs. R: “Please Don’t Tell Her”
text:
Back to Mrs. R: “Please Don’t Tell Her”

The biopsy is worse than expected. Before you go in, Dana stops you in the hallway:

“In our family, we don’t tell elders news like this. It will crush her. Please talk to me, not her.”

The team wants to respect the family’s culture. The team also wants to respect Mrs. R.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Thought Question


Whose decision is this — Mrs. R’s, Dana’s, or the team’s?
What is the first thing you should find out, and who do you ask?
What if you ask Mrs. R and she says, “I don’t want the details — talk to Dana”?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Make It Contemporary

A genetic test shows Mrs. R carries a mutation her adult siblings may also carry.

She does not want them told. They may be making medical decisions without information that could protect them.
Does her privacy outweigh their need to know? Is this the same problem as the family request, or a different one?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note



Only Mrs. R can waive her own right to know. A competent patient may autonomously choose not to receive information (“just talk to my daughter”) — that is respected autonomy. The family withholding it from her, unasked, is not their call.
So it isn’t “autonomy vs. culture.” The move is to ask Mrs. R — before the bad news — how much she wants to know and whom she wants involved. Culturally informed, and still hers.
The genetic case is different in kind: a third party’s welfare is now at stake. That is closer to Tarasoff than to truth-telling.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Mrs. R, Start to Finish

You met her as a frightened patient with a daughter who spoke for her
You chose a model with her, not for her
You made consent a conversation — and took her tears seriously without calling them incapacity
You kept her information private by default — and asked her, not Dana, how she wanted the truth handled

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Two Mistakes to Avoid

Reducing autonomy to “the customer picks from a menu”
Treating every bad or emotional decision as proof of incapacity
Treating HIPAA as a gag order
Assuming one cultural script fits every patient

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Paired Case: Dax Cowart

Read for next class: Case 1 — Dax Cowart


Severe burns and unbearable pain
Repeated refusal of treatment
Family, physicians, and patient pulling in different directions
One of the clearest tests of autonomy in clinical ethics

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Wrapping Up Part B

Part A explained why modern medicine rejects unchecked paternalism. Part B asked what replaces it: guided, transparent, autonomy-respecting care. Following Mrs. R, we saw the four clinician-patient models, the four elements of valid consent, the difference between an unwise choice and an incapacitated one, and confidentiality and truth-telling as defaults with disciplined exceptions.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
References


Appelbaum, Paul S. 2007. “Assessment of Patients’ Competence to Consent to Treatment.” New England Journal of Medicine 357 (18): 1834–40. https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMcp074045.


Cowart, Dax, and Robert Burt. 1998. “Confronting Death: Who Chooses, Who Controls?” Hastings Center Report 28 (1): 14–24. https://doi.org/10.2307/3528337.


Faden, Ruth R., and Tom L. Beauchamp. 1986. A History and Theory of Informed Consent. Oxford University Press.


Supreme Court of California. 1976. Tarasoff v. Regents of the University of California, 17 Cal. 3d 425 (1976). Supreme Court of California decision.


U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, Office for Civil Rights. 2025. Summary of the HIPAA Privacy Rule. HHS. https://www.hhs.gov/hipaa/for-professionals/privacy/laws-regulations/index.html.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
In Part A we asked: what are the four principles, and how do you read a moral argument?
Part B asks the next question:

Where do the principles come from — and how do we weigh them when they conflict?

That is where ethical theory comes in.

By the end of Part B, you will be able to:

State the core commitment of utilitarianism, Kantian ethics, virtue ethics, and care ethics
Explain how each theory grounds or prioritizes the four principles differently
Apply at least two theories to the same clinical case and show they yield different answers

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
In Part A we asked: what are the four principles, and how do you read a moral argument?
Part B asks the next question:

Where do the principles come from — and how do we weigh them when they conflict?

That is where ethical theory comes in.

By the end of Part B, you will be able to:

State the core commitment of utilitarianism, Kantian ethics, virtue ethics, and care ethics
Explain how each theory grounds or prioritizes the four principles differently
Apply at least two theories to the same clinical case and show they yield different answers

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
1.1 What Theories Do
The four principles — autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, justice — give us a shared vocabulary. But they do not, by themselves, tell us which principle to prioritize when they conflict (Beauchamp and Childress 2019).
Ethical theories fill that gap. Think of them as lenses:

Each focuses on something real
Each has blind spots
No single lens sees everything

We will use all four throughout the course — not to pick a winner, but to see more of the problem.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
1.2 A Roadmap








Theory
Core question
Key figure




Utilitarianism
What produces the best outcomes?
Mill, Bentham


Kantian ethics
What are my duties, regardless of outcome?
Kant


Virtue ethics
What would a person of good character do?
Aristotle


Care ethics
What do my relationships and context require?
Gilligan, Held




Remind students: these will appear in almost every subsequent lecture. Abortion, end of life, justice — each one sees these theories pulling differently.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
2.1 Mill and Bentham




J. S. Mill, c. 1870. London Stereoscopic Co. Public domain.




Jeremy Bentham, by H. W. Pickersgill. Public domain.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
2.3 The Greatest Happiness Principle
Utilitarianism judges actions entirely by their consequences:

What matters is welfare — happiness, well-being, the absence of suffering
All individuals count equally; no one gets extra weight
The morally required action is the one that maximizes total welfare

Bentham called this the hedonic calculus — weigh the pleasures and pains produced, add them up, choose the highest score (Bentham 1789).

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
2.4 Utilitarianism and the Four Principles
Beneficence is central — acting to maximize welfare just is acting beneficently.
Justice emerges from impartiality: each person counts as one, so unfair distributions that reduce total welfare are wrong.
Autonomy is valued instrumentally — respecting autonomy usually maximizes welfare — but can be overridden when it doesn’t.
Non-Maleficence is captured under the harm side of the ledger.






Note



The tension. A strict utilitarian could justify deceiving a patient if the deception produces better outcomes. Most clinicians resist this — which is why other theories matter.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
2.5 Utilitarianism: Weighing Harms and Benefits







util


Q

Which option produces
better expected outcomes?



A

Drug A
(aggressive)



Q->A





B

Drug B
(conservative)



Q->B





EA

Recovery: likely, benefit large
Side effects: unlikely, harm serious
→ Weighted benefit: HIGH



A->EA





EB

Recovery: possible, benefit moderate
Side effects: likely, harm minor
→ Weighted benefit: MODERATE



B->EB





R

Prescribe Drug A
(higher weighted benefit)



EA->R





EB->R






 Utilitarian decision procedure: for each option, estimate the probability and magnitude of every likely outcome (benefits and harms), sum the weighted results, and choose the option with the highest overall expected benefit.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
2.6 Thought Question

A city has 10 ventilators and 15 patients who need one to survive. Five of the fifteen have a much higher probability of surviving with ventilator support.
A utilitarian protocol would allocate ventilators to maximize lives saved — which likely means the five highest-probability patients get priority.

Is that fair to the other ten patients?
Does it matter why some patients have lower survival odds — age, disability, underlying illness?
What would the principle of justice say that utilitarianism might miss?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
2.7 After You’ve Discussed






Note



The utilitarian reply. A sophisticated utilitarian can say: the protocol maximizes welfare across the group, which is what justice requires. The critique is that it treats some lives as worth less, and that the reasons behind lower survival odds (disability, chronic illness) are often tied to existing inequities. We will return to this in L9 (Public Health Ethics) and L8 (Justice).

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.1 Kant




Immanuel Kant, painted portrait. Public domain (Wikimedia Commons).


Immanuel Kant (1724–1804) argued that morality is grounded in reason, not consequences.
The key test is his Categorical Imperative — a rule for whether an action is morally permissible, applied before you look at outcomes.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.2 The Humanity Formulation
The most clinically relevant form of the Categorical Imperative (Kant 1785):


“Now I say: man and generally any rational being exists as an end in himself, not merely as a means to be arbitrarily used by this or that will.… So act as to treat humanity, whether in thine own person or in that of any other, in every case as an end withal, never as means only.”


Kant, Groundwork of the Metaphysic of Morals (1785), trans. T. K. Abbott (Kant 1785)

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: 3.4 Kantian Ethics and the Four Principles
text:
3.4 Kantian Ethics and the Four Principles
Autonomy is Kant’s central principle — respecting autonomy is what it means to treat persons as ends.
Non-Maleficence reflects the duty not to harm persons who have inherent dignity.
Beneficence is a genuine duty, but cannot override autonomy — benefiting someone by deceiving them is still wrong.
Justice flows from treating persons equally as ends — no one’s dignity is worth more than another’s.






Note



Contrast with utilitarianism. Kant would say: you must never lie to the Jehovah’s Witness patient about her prognosis to get her to consent, even if lying would save her life.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: 3.5 Kantian Ethics: Perfect and Imperfect Duties
text:
3.5 Kantian Ethics: Perfect and Imperfect Duties







kant


A

Proposed clinical action
(e.g., withhold
bad news to spare distress)



Q

Perfect duties
(humanity principle):
Respect autonomy?
Avoid harm?
Treat justly?



A->Q





W

IMPERMISSIBLE
Patient treated as
mere means



Q->W


Any violated



Imp

Imperfect duty:
benefit / aid the patient
(must be fulfilled;
how is flexible)



Q->Imp


All pass



How

Deliberate:
how to best fulfill
this duty here



Imp->How





Act

ACT
(latitude in method,
timing, approach)



How->Act






 Perfect duties (amber, from the humanity principle) are absolute constraints checked first — any violation makes the action impermissible. The imperfect duty to benefit the patient comes after: it must be fulfilled in some form, but the clinician has latitude in how.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.6 Thought Question

A patient with severe depression has been refusing an antidepressant. His psychiatrist suspects the refusal is itself a symptom of the illness — that his depression is preventing him from appreciating how treatment could help him.

Does the Kantian view help here — or complicate it?
When, if ever, does treating the illness require overriding the patient’s expressed wishes?
How is this different from the Jehovah’s Witness case?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.7 After You’ve Discussed






Note



The Kantian tension. Kant’s framework requires that the patient be capable of rational self-governance for autonomy to apply fully. If the illness itself impairs that capacity, overriding expressed wishes may be consistent with treating him as an end — but this reasoning can also be abused to justify paternalism. Decision-making capacity assessment (L2) is how clinical ethics tries to hold this line carefully.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.1 Aristotle and Eudaimonia




Aristotle (copy of Lysippos bust). Palazzo Altemps. Public domain.


Aristotle asked not “what should I do?” but “what kind of person should I be?” (Aristotle 340 BC)
Eudaimonia — often translated flourishing — is the goal of human life: living and acting in accordance with the virtues of a fully developed human being.
A virtue is a stable character trait — developed through practice — that reliably disposes a person to act and feel well.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: 4.2 The Virtues of the Good Nurse
text:
4.2 The Virtues of the Good Nurse
Virtue ethics is especially useful in professional ethics, because professions define role-specific excellences.
The ANA Code of Ethics (American Nurses Association 2015) describes the good nurse as someone who embodies:

Compassion — genuine concern for the patient’s suffering
Integrity — consistency between values and actions, even under pressure
Fidelity — keeping commitments made to patients and profession
Practical wisdom (phronesis) — knowing how to apply general principles to particular situations

These are not policies. They are traits you develop by practicing them.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: 4.3 Virtue Ethics and the Four Principles
text:
4.3 Virtue Ethics and the Four Principles
Virtue ethics asks: what would a person of good character do in this situation?
Beneficence — the virtuous clinician genuinely cares about the patient’s good, not just follows protocols.
Non-Maleficence — integrity means honesty about what you can and cannot do.
Autonomy — respect for persons flows from compassion and fidelity, not just rules.
Justice — fairness requires the virtue of impartiality even toward patients you find difficult.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: 4.4 Virtue Ethics: The Cycle of Character Development
text:
4.4 Virtue Ethics: The Cycle of Character Development







virtue


A

Ask: what would a
virtuous nurse do?



B

Deliberate:
what does this
patient need?



A->B





C

Act with compassion,
integrity, fidelity



B->C





D

Reflect on outcomes
and relationships



C->D





E

Character deepens;
phronesis grows



D->E





E->A


more natural
over time




 Virtue ethics is developmental — good character is built through repeated intentional action, not rule-following. The dashed return edge marks the cycle: over time, virtuous action becomes more natural and less effortful.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: 5.1 Gilligan and the Ethics of Care
text:
5.1 Gilligan and the Ethics of Care
Carol Gilligan (Gilligan 1982) argued that dominant moral theories — utilitarian and Kantian — were built around abstract rules applied to strangers. They missed what actually structures most moral life: relationships, dependency, and the responsibility that comes with them.

“[T]he moral problem arises from conflicting responsibilities rather than from competing rights and requires for its resolution a mode of thinking that is contextual and narrative rather than formal and abstract.” — Gilligan, In a Different Voice (1982)

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.2 Relationships, Context, Vulnerability
Care ethics, developed by Gilligan and later Virginia Held (Held 2006), asks:

Who is this patient, in this situation, with this set of relationships?
What do I owe them given the relationship I have with them?
Whose needs are being made invisible by abstract rule-following?

In nursing practice, care ethics is not a soft add-on. It describes what nurses already know: the moral weight of the bedside relationship cannot be reduced to a checklist.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: 5.3 Care Ethics and the Four Principles
text:
5.3 Care Ethics and the Four Principles
Autonomy is reframed: rather than the isolated individual making a formal choice, it is the relational self — embedded in family, community, culture.
Beneficence is particularized: it matters what this person needs, not what the average patient needs.
Non-Maleficence includes attending to emotional harm and the harm of not being seen or heard.
Justice requires attending to who is made vulnerable by social structures — and who gets left out of abstract policy.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: 5.4 Care Ethics: The Web of Relationships
text:
5.4 Care Ethics: The Web of Relationships







care


N

Nurse /
Clinician



C

Patient
(clinical situation)



N->C




T

team



T->C




Pr

obligations



Pr->C




F

loved ones



C->F

& culture

 Care ethics centers the patient within a web of concrete relationships. Moral obligations arise from these particular connections — not from abstract rules applied to strangers. The nurse–patient relationship (bold edge) is the immediate site of care.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.5 Thought Question

A 78-year-old woman with mild cognitive impairment makes decisions that worry her adult children. She insists on living alone; she sometimes forgets meals; she declines the assisted living facility they have arranged.

A utilitarian might look at her risk profile and long-term welfare. A Kantian might ask about her decision-making capacity. A virtue ethicist might ask what a caring daughter or a virtuous clinician would do.
What does the care ethics lens add that the others miss?
Is there anything it might obscure?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
5.6 After You’ve Discussed






Note



What care ethics adds. It centers the relationship and the patient’s embeddedness in family and community — her story, not just her risk score. What it can obscure: the caring family can also be a controlling one; care ethics needs to ask whether the relationships are themselves just. This is one reason the four principles framework is used alongside the theories rather than replaced by any single one.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
6.1 The Theories as Lenses








Theory
Foundational commitment
Role of the four principles




Utilitarianism
Maximize welfare
Principles are derived — follow them because doing so usually produces the best outcomes


Kantian ethics
Respect persons as ends
Principles are duties grounded in rational dignity — especially autonomy


Virtue ethics
Cultivate good character
Principles are expressions of virtue — practical wisdom tells you how to apply them


Care ethics
Honor relationships
Principles are contextualized by relationship and dependency — not applied abstractly

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
6.2 Which Theory Is Right?
Probably none of them — completely. Each illuminates something the others miss:

Utilitarianism sees the population and the aggregate.
Kantian ethics sees the dignity of the individual that cannot be traded away.
Virtue ethics sees the character of the clinician and the quality of the relationship.
Care ethics sees the context and dependency that abstract rules can erase.

The four principles work in part because they are theory-agnostic: a Kantian and a utilitarian can both endorse autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, and justice — they just disagree about why and how to weight them.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
6.3 Wrapping Up Lecture 1

Bioethics uses a four-principle framework as its shared vocabulary, supported by four major ethical theories that offer different answers to the question of how to weigh those principles. Utilitarianism focuses on outcomes and aggregate welfare. Kantian ethics focuses on duties and the dignity of persons. Virtue ethics focuses on character. Care ethics focuses on relationships and context. No single theory is correct; used as lenses, they let us see more of what is morally at stake in any clinical situation.
Lecture 2 turns to the history that gave these principles their urgency, and the clinical relationship where they are daily tested.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Bridge from Part A
In Part A we asked: what are the four principles, and how do you read a moral argument?
Part B asks the next question:

Where do the principles come from — and how do we weigh them when they conflict?

That is where ethical theory comes in.

By the end of Part B, you will be able to:

State the core commitment of utilitarianism, Kantian ethics, virtue ethics, and care ethics
Explain how each theory grounds or prioritizes the four principles differently
Apply at least two theories to the same clinical case and show they yield different answers

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
What Theories Do
The four principles — autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, justice — give us a shared vocabulary. But they do not, by themselves, tell us which principle to prioritize when they conflict (Beauchamp and Childress 2019).
Ethical theories fill that gap. Think of them as lenses:

Each focuses on something real
Each has blind spots
No single lens sees everything

We will use all four throughout the course — not to pick a winner, but to see more of the problem.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Mill and Bentham




J. S. Mill, c. 1870. London Stereoscopic Co. Public domain.




Jeremy Bentham, by H. W. Pickersgill. Public domain.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
The Greatest Happiness Principle
Utilitarianism judges actions entirely by their consequences:

What matters is welfare — happiness, well-being, the absence of suffering
All individuals count equally; no one gets extra weight
The morally required action is the one that maximizes total welfare

Bentham called this the hedonic calculus — weigh the pleasures and pains produced, add them up, choose the highest score (Bentham 1789).

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Utilitarianism and the Four Principles
Beneficence is central — acting to maximize welfare just is acting beneficently.
Justice emerges from impartiality: each person counts as one, so unfair distributions that reduce total welfare are wrong.
Autonomy is valued instrumentally — respecting autonomy usually maximizes welfare — but can be overridden when it doesn’t.
Non-Maleficence is captured under the harm side of the ledger.






Note


The tension. A strict utilitarian could justify deceiving a patient if the deception produces better outcomes. Most clinicians resist this — which is why other theories matter.

Side effects: unlikely, harm serious

(higher weighted benefit)

 Utilitarian decision procedure: for each option, estimate the probability and magnitude of every likely outcome (benefits and harms), sum the weighted results, and choose the option with the highest overall expected benefit.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Thought Question

A city has 10 ventilators and 15 patients who need one to survive. Five of the fifteen have a much higher probability of surviving with ventilator support.
A utilitarian protocol would allocate ventilators to maximize lives saved — which likely means the five highest-probability patients get priority.

Is that fair to the other ten patients?
Does it matter why some patients have lower survival odds — age, disability, underlying illness?
What would the principle of justice say that utilitarianism might miss?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


The utilitarian reply. A sophisticated utilitarian can say: the protocol maximizes welfare across the group, which is what justice requires. The critique is that it treats some lives as worth less, and that the reasons behind lower survival odds (disability, chronic illness) are often tied to existing inequities. We will return to this in L9 (Public Health Ethics) and L8 (Justice).

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Kant




Immanuel Kant, painted portrait. Public domain (Wikimedia Commons).


Immanuel Kant (1724–1804) argued that morality is grounded in reason, not consequences.
The key test is his Categorical Imperative — a rule for whether an action is morally permissible, applied before you look at outcomes.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
The Humanity Formulation
The most clinically relevant form of the Categorical Imperative (Kant 1785):


“Now I say: man and generally any rational being exists as an end in himself, not merely as a means to be arbitrarily used by this or that will.… So act as to treat humanity, whether in thine own person or in that of any other, in every case as an end withal, never as means only.”


Kant, Groundwork of the Metaphysic of Morals (1785), trans. T. K. Abbott (Kant 1785)

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: Kantian Ethics and the Four Principles
text:
Kantian Ethics and the Four Principles
Autonomy is Kant’s central principle — respecting autonomy is what it means to treat persons as ends.
Non-Maleficence reflects the duty not to harm persons who have inherent dignity.
Beneficence is a genuine duty, but cannot override autonomy — benefiting someone by deceiving them is still wrong.
Justice flows from treating persons equally as ends — no one’s dignity is worth more than another’s.






Note


Contrast with utilitarianism. Kant would say: you must never lie to the Jehovah’s Witness patient about her prognosis to get her to consent, even if lying would save her life.

kant

 Perfect duties (amber, from the humanity principle) are absolute constraints checked first — any violation makes the action impermissible. The imperfect duty to benefit the patient comes after: it must be fulfilled in some form, but the clinician has latitude in how.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Thought Question

A patient with severe depression has been refusing an antidepressant. His psychiatrist suspects the refusal is itself a symptom of the illness — that his depression is preventing him from appreciating how treatment could help him.

Does the Kantian view help here — or complicate it?
When, if ever, does treating the illness require overriding the patient’s expressed wishes?
How is this different from the Jehovah’s Witness case?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


The Kantian tension. Kant’s framework requires that the patient be capable of rational self-governance for autonomy to apply fully. If the illness itself impairs that capacity, overriding expressed wishes may be consistent with treating him as an end — but this reasoning can also be abused to justify paternalism. Decision-making capacity assessment (L2) is how clinical ethics tries to hold this line carefully.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Aristotle and Eudaimonia




Aristotle (copy of Lysippos bust). Palazzo Altemps. Public domain.


Aristotle asked not “what should I do?” but “what kind of person should I be?” (Aristotle 340 BC)
Eudaimonia — often translated flourishing — is the goal of human life: living and acting in accordance with the virtues of a fully developed human being.
A virtue is a stable character trait — developed through practice — that reliably disposes a person to act and feel well.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: The Virtues of the Good Nurse
text:
The Virtues of the Good Nurse
Virtue ethics is especially useful in professional ethics, because professions define role-specific excellences.
The ANA Code of Ethics (American Nurses Association 2015) describes the good nurse as someone who embodies:

Compassion — genuine concern for the patient’s suffering
Integrity — consistency between values and actions, even under pressure
Fidelity — keeping commitments made to patients and profession
Practical wisdom (phronesis) — knowing how to apply general principles to particular situations

These are not policies. They are traits you develop by practicing them.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: Virtue Ethics and the Four Principles
text:
Virtue Ethics and the Four Principles
Virtue ethics asks: what would a person of good character do in this situation?
Beneficence — the virtuous clinician genuinely cares about the patient’s good, not just follows protocols.
Non-Maleficence — integrity means honesty about what you can and cannot do.
Autonomy — respect for persons flows from compassion and fidelity, not just rules.
Justice — fairness requires the virtue of impartiality even toward patients you find difficult.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: Gilligan and the Ethics of Care
text:
Gilligan and the Ethics of Care
Carol Gilligan (Gilligan 1982) argued that dominant moral theories — utilitarian and Kantian — were built around abstract rules applied to strangers. They missed what actually structures most moral life: relationships, dependency, and the responsibility that comes with them.

“[T]he moral problem arises from conflicting responsibilities rather than from competing rights and requires for its resolution a mode of thinking that is contextual and narrative rather than formal and abstract.” — Gilligan, In a Different Voice (1982)

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Relationships, Context, Vulnerability
Care ethics, developed by Gilligan and later Virginia Held (Held 2006), asks:

Who is this patient, in this situation, with this set of relationships?
What do I owe them given the relationship I have with them?
Whose needs are being made invisible by abstract rule-following?

In nursing practice, care ethics is not a soft add-on. It describes what nurses already know: the moral weight of the bedside relationship cannot be reduced to a checklist.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: Care Ethics and the Four Principles
text:
Care Ethics and the Four Principles
Autonomy is reframed: rather than the isolated individual making a formal choice, it is the relational self — embedded in family, community, culture.
Beneficence is particularized: it matters what this person needs, not what the average patient needs.
Non-Maleficence includes attending to emotional harm and the harm of not being seen or heard.
Justice requires attending to who is made vulnerable by social structures — and who gets left out of abstract policy.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Thought Question

A 78-year-old woman with mild cognitive impairment makes decisions that worry her adult children. She insists on living alone; she sometimes forgets meals; she declines the assisted living facility they have arranged.

A utilitarian might look at her risk profile and long-term welfare. A Kantian might ask about her decision-making capacity. A virtue ethicist might ask what a caring daughter or a virtuous clinician would do.
What does the care ethics lens add that the others miss?
Is there anything it might obscure?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


What care ethics adds. It centers the relationship and the patient’s embeddedness in family and community — her story, not just her risk score. What it can obscure: the caring family can also be a controlling one; care ethics needs to ask whether the relationships are themselves just. This is one reason the four principles framework is used alongside the theories rather than replaced by any single one.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
The Theories as Lenses








Theory
Foundational commitment
Role of the four principles




Utilitarianism
Maximize welfare
Principles are derived — follow them because doing so usually produces the best outcomes


Kantian ethics
Respect persons as ends
Principles are duties grounded in rational dignity — especially autonomy


Virtue ethics
Cultivate good character
Principles are expressions of virtue — practical wisdom tells you how to apply them


Care ethics
Honor relationships
Principles are contextualized by relationship and dependency — not applied abstractly

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Which Theory Is Right?
Probably none of them — completely. Each illuminates something the others miss:

Utilitarianism sees the population and the aggregate.
Kantian ethics sees the dignity of the individual that cannot be traded away.
Virtue ethics sees the character of the clinician and the quality of the relationship.
Care ethics sees the context and dependency that abstract rules can erase.

The four principles work in part because they are theory-agnostic: a Kantian and a utilitarian can both endorse autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, and justice — they just disagree about why and how to weight them.
