Lecture 2 turns to the history that gave these principles their urgency, and the clinical relationship where they are daily tested.

title: Course Outline & Learning Outcomes
text:
This course consists of 12 lectures, each delivered in two parts (designed for a twice-weekly schedule). Online students watch both parts as a single unit. Alongside the lectures, students read and discuss 12 case study documents drawn from historical and contemporary bioethics.
The course is organized around the four principles of biomedical ethics — autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, and justice. Major ethical theories (utilitarianism, Kantian ethics, virtue ethics, care ethics, principlism) are introduced as lenses that ground and sometimes conflict over these principles.

title: Course Outline & Learning Outcomes
text:
0.2 Course-Level Learning Outcomes
By the end of the course, students will be able to:

Identify and apply the four principles of biomedical ethics (autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, justice) to clinical and research scenarios.
Analyze moral arguments — identifying premises, conclusions, hidden assumptions, and common fallacies.
Compare how major ethical frameworks (utilitarian, Kantian, virtue, care, principlist) approach the same dilemma and yield different conclusions.
Evaluate real and contemporary bioethics cases using appropriate legal, historical, and empirical context.
Articulate their own reasoned position on a contested bioethical issue, engaging fairly with opposing views.

title: Course Outline & Learning Outcomes
text:
0.4 Lecture-Level Learning Outcomes

0.4.1 L1. Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning

Define the four principles of biomedical ethics and give a clinical example of each.
Distinguish ethical claims from legal, religious, or personal-preference claims.
Identify the premises and conclusion of a moral argument written in ordinary prose.
Recognize common fallacies in bioethical reasoning (ad hominem, appeal to nature, slippery slope, naturalistic fallacy).
Summarize the core commitments of utilitarianism, Kantian ethics, virtue ethics, and care ethics.
Explain how each theory grounds or prioritizes the four principles differently.

title: Course Outline & Learning Outcomes
text:
1.1 L2. History & the Clinical Relationship

1.1.1 L2a. From Hippocrates to the Belmont Report: A History

The Hippocratic tradition: “first, do no harm,” confidentiality, and what the original oath actually said (vs. what people think it said).
Pre-WWII medical ethics: paternalism as the default, eugenics movements in the US and Europe.
Nazi medical experiments and the Nuremberg Trials (1945–47).
The Nuremberg Code (1947): voluntary consent as the first principle.
Tuskegee Syphilis Study (1932–72): what happened, why it took 40 years to stop, and the public response.
The Belmont Report (1979) and the rise of bioethics as a discipline.
Why “ethics committees” and IRBs exist today.

Visual elements: - Image (public domain): Hippocrates bust, Wellcome Collection / Wikimedia Commons. - Image (public domain): Nuremberg Doctors’ Trial photograph (US National Archives). - Image (public domain): Tuskegee study photo from CDC archives (the well-known photo of a doctor drawing blood from a subject). - Figure (code-generated): timeline of bioethics milestones, 1900–2000, with key events as labeled markers. - Table: Side-by-side comparison of Nuremberg Code (1947), Declaration of Helsinki (1964), and Belmont Report (1979) — date, audience, key principles. - Sidebar: “Where does the word ‘bioethics’ come from?” (Van Rensselaer Potter, 1970).


1.1.2 L2b. The Clinical Relationship: Consent, Autonomy, and Confidentiality

The four Emanuel & Emanuel models of the clinician-patient relationship:

Paternalistic (“doctor knows best”)
Informative (“here are the facts; you decide”)
Interpretive (“let me help you figure out what you want”)
Deliberative (“let’s talk about what you should want”)

The four elements of valid informed consent: disclosure, understanding, voluntariness, capacity.
When autonomy and beneficence conflict (e.g., the patient refusing a life-saving transfusion).
Confidentiality and its limits: HIPAA basics, mandatory reporting (child abuse, certain infectious diseases), and the Tarasoff duty-to-warn case (1976).
Truth-telling across cultures: when families ask the doctor not to tell the patient their diagnosis.

Visual elements: - Figure (code-generated): 2×2 grid placing the four Emanuel models along axes of “who sets values?” and “who weighs options?” - Figure (code-generated): flowchart for the four elements of informed consent (a clinician’s mental checklist). - Table: Examples of when confidentiality may or must be broken (mandatory reporting, Tarasoff, public health, subpoena), with US legal basis. - Case box: The Tarasoff case — short narrative, then discussion questions. - Case box: A cross-cultural truth-telling scenario (e.g., family of an elderly patient with a new cancer diagnosis asks the team not to tell the patient).

title: Course Outline & Learning Outcomes
text:
1.2 L3. Research Ethics

1.2.1 L3a. How Clinical Research Works (and Why It’s Different from Care)

What a randomized controlled trial is: randomization, blinding (single, double), placebo, control group, intention-to-treat analysis.
Phases of clinical trials (I → IV) in plain language.
The crucial distinction: research aims to produce generalizable knowledge; clinical care aims to benefit this patient. The same person doing both creates tension.
The therapeutic misconception: when participants believe an experimental treatment is being chosen for their benefit.
Why volunteers in early-phase trials face risk with little personal upside.

Visual elements: - Figure (code-generated): diagram of a parallel-group RCT — participants enrolled, randomized, two arms (treatment / placebo), followed to outcome. - Figure (code-generated): funnel showing trial phases I–IV with typical sample sizes and goals at each phase. - Table: Research vs. clinical care — goals, who benefits, who pays, what governs it (IRB vs. medical license). - Sidebar: “What does ‘double-blind’ actually mean?” with a small example.


1.2.2 L3b. From Tuskegee to Today: Protecting Research Subjects

Three case studies and what each taught us:

Tuskegee — race, deception, withholding treatment.
Willowbrook (1956–70) — hepatitis studies on children with intellectual disabilities.
Nazi experiments — coerced participation in concentration camps.

The three Belmont principles: respect for persons, beneficence, justice — and how each maps to a real IRB protection (consent, risk-benefit, fair subject selection).
Vulnerable populations: children, prisoners, pregnant people, those with cognitive impairment, economically disadvantaged groups — and what extra protections apply.
Placebo controls when an effective treatment already exists: is it ever ethical?
Research in low-resource settings: the short-course AZT trials in sub-Saharan Africa (1990s) as a case study.

Visual elements: - Image (public domain): Willowbrook State School exterior photograph (NY State Archives / Wikimedia Commons, public domain). - Figure (code-generated): mapping of Belmont principles → IRB protections (three-column diagram). - Table: Vulnerable populations and the specific protections required for each. - Case box: The AZT trials debate — Lurie & Wolfe vs. Varmus & Satcher, with discussion prompts. - Discussion prompt: “Would you join a Phase I trial for $5,000? For a friend? For your child?”

title: Course Outline & Learning Outcomes
text:
1.3 L4. Abortion

1.3.1 L4a. Moral Status and the Major Philosophical Arguments

A short biology primer: zygote → embryo → fetus → viability → term.
Four candidate criteria for moral status: biological humanity, personhood, sentience, viability — what each implies.
Thomson’s violinist argument (1971): bodily autonomy even granting fetal personhood.
Marquis’s future-like-ours argument (1989): why killing a fetus is wrong for the same reason killing an adult is.
Feminist and reproductive justice perspectives (Roberts, Ross): why “choice” framing is incomplete without access, economics, and race.

Visual elements: - Figure (code-generated): timeline of fetal development with markers at key developmental milestones (heartbeat detectable, neural development, viability) — neutrally labeled. - Figure (code-generated): concept map of the four moral status criteria and which views each supports. - Table: Thomson’s violinist vs. Marquis’s future-like-ours — what each argues, what it concedes, common objections. - Discussion prompt: “Is the violinist analogy a fair comparison? Where does it break down?”


1.3.2 L4b. Law, Cases, and the Four Principles

Roe v. Wade (1973), Planned Parenthood v. Casey (1992), and Dobbs v. Jackson (2022): a brief legal arc.
The post-Dobbs landscape: state-by-state variation in 2024–25, including trigger laws, gestational limits, and exceptions.
Applying the four principles (autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, justice) to specific cases:

Pregnancy from rape
Severe fetal anomaly incompatible with life
Maternal life endangered (e.g., ectopic pregnancy, severe preeclampsia)
Early vs. late gestation

How exceptions function (or fail) in practice: documented cases of clinicians unwilling to act under legal ambiguity.

Visual elements: - Figure (code-generated): US map showing state abortion laws as of the most recent data the textbook can cite (with footnote that this changes). - Table: Four principles applied across four sample cases (rape, anomaly, maternal life, elective). - Case box: A real reported case of delayed care under restrictive law (e.g., the documented Texas cases from 2023–24). - Sidebar: “What does ‘exception for the life of the mother’ actually mean in a hospital?”

title: Course Outline & Learning Outcomes
text:
1.4 L5. Reproductive Technology

1.4.1 L5a. The Technology and What It Lets Us Do

IVF (in vitro fertilization): step-by-step in accessible language.
ICSI (intracytoplasmic sperm injection) — when and why it’s used.
PGD/PGT (preimplantation genetic diagnosis/testing): testing embryos before implantation.
Gestational surrogacy: who’s involved, who’s the legal parent, where it’s legal.
The newer frontier: synthetic gametes (in vitro gametogenesis), posthumous reproduction (sperm/egg retrieval after death).

Visual elements: - Figure (code-generated): step-by-step IVF diagram (egg retrieval → fertilization → embryo culture → transfer). - Figure (code-generated): PGD/PGT decision tree (test for what? select for what?). - Table: IVF success rates by age band (using CDC ART data, cited). - Sidebar: “What happens to leftover embryos?” — a practical look at the ~1 million frozen embryos in US storage.


1.4.2 L5b. Ethical Debates: Selection, Commerce, and Family

Embryo selection and where lines get drawn:

Selecting against serious genetic disease (e.g., Huntington’s, Tay-Sachs)
Sex selection for “family balancing”
“Savior siblings” selected as tissue donors for an existing child
Selecting against disability (links to L11)

Commercial surrogacy and egg donation: Anderson’s commodification critique vs. Robertson’s procreative liberty.
Cross-border reproductive care (“fertility tourism”) and exploitation concerns.
Applying the four principles to embryo selection cases.

Visual elements: - Table: Embryo selection scenarios — what’s being selected, who benefits, what’s contested. - Figure (code-generated): spectrum diagram, “more medical ↔︎ more elective” for selection traits. - Case box: The Nash family savior-sibling case (Adam Nash, 2000) — facts and discussion. - Discussion prompt: “Is paying $50,000 for an egg from an Ivy League donor different from paying $5,000 for an egg from a typical donor? Why or why not?”

title: Course Outline & Learning Outcomes
text:
1.5 L6. Genetics & Enhancement

1.5.1 L6a. Genetics Basics and What CRISPR Changes

Vocabulary every student needs: gene, genome, allele, chromosome, DNA, mutation.
Germline vs. somatic cells — and why this distinction matters morally.
Patterns of inheritance: dominant, recessive, X-linked (using one familiar example each, e.g., Huntington’s, cystic fibrosis, hemophilia).
CRISPR-Cas9 in accessible terms: a programmable “find and replace” for DNA.
Gene therapy vs. genetic enhancement — and whether that line really holds up.

Visual elements: - Figure (code-generated): gene-to-genome zoom diagram (nucleotide → gene → chromosome → genome). - Figure (code-generated): CRISPR-Cas9 mechanism illustration — guide RNA, Cas9, target sequence, cut. - Image (public domain): DNA double helix diagram (NIH / NHGRI public domain images). - Table: Examples of somatic gene therapies approved as of recent FDA data (e.g., Casgevy for sickle cell, Luxturna for retinal dystrophy). - Sidebar: “Therapy or enhancement?” — short examples that resist easy classification (e.g., editing for higher-than-average disease resistance).


1.5.2 L6b. Editing Humans: The He Jiankui Case and the Enhancement Debate

The He Jiankui case (2018): what he did to the CCR5 gene in twin embryos, why the scientific community condemned it, what happened to him.
The international response and current consensus on germline editing.
The non-identity problem (Parfit): can you harm someone by choosing the conditions of their existence?
The “child’s right to an open future” (Feinberg).
The enhancement debate:

Savulescu: “procreative beneficence” — parents have reason to select the best possible child.
Sandel: enhancement reflects a “drive to mastery” that corrodes appreciation of life as gift.
Glannon: distinguishing acceptable from unacceptable enhancements.


Visual elements: - Image (public domain or fair-use news photo, cited): He Jiankui at a 2018 conference (note: confirm public domain status — fallback to a code-generated diagram of the CCR5 edit). - Figure (code-generated): timeline of the He Jiankui case from 2017 announcement to 2019 prison sentence. - Table: Savulescu vs. Sandel vs. Glannon on enhancement — what each accepts, rejects, and why. - Discussion prompt: “If parents could safely give their child a 10-point IQ boost, should they?” - Case box: The non-identity problem in a concrete form — “the 14-year-old girl” thought experiment.

title: Course Outline & Learning Outcomes
text:
1.6 L7. End of Life

1.6.1 L7a. Death, Dying, and Advance Planning

Defining the end of life clinically: brain death, coma, persistent vegetative state, minimally conscious state — what’s different about each.
The 1968 Harvard criteria and the move to whole-brain death.
Advance directives in everyday terms:

Living wills
Durable power of attorney for health care
POLST / MOLST forms (and why these are different from a living will)

Surrogate decision-making standards:

Substituted judgment (“what would this patient have wanted?”)
Best interest (“what’s best for someone in this situation?”)

High-profile cases: Karen Ann Quinlan (1976), Nancy Cruzan (1990), Terri Schiavo (1990–2005).

Visual elements: - Figure (code-generated): spectrum diagram of consciousness states from coma to full awareness, with diagnostic criteria for each. - Figure (code-generated): flowchart, “Who decides?” — patient → designated proxy → next of kin hierarchy, with state variation note. - Table: Living will vs. durable power of attorney vs. POLST — what each is, who fills it out, when it kicks in. - Case box: The Schiavo case in brief — timeline, legal battles, what made it different from Quinlan and Cruzan. - Sidebar: A sample advance directive form (simplified, illustrative).


1.6.2 L7b. Euthanasia, Assisted Death, and Their Critics

A vocabulary that students consistently confuse:

Active euthanasia (administering a lethal substance)
Passive euthanasia (withholding or withdrawing treatment)
Withdrawal of treatment (not the same as killing)
Physician-assisted death / MAID (prescribing, not administering)

Rachels’ argument (1975): the active/passive distinction doesn’t carry the moral weight people think it does.
The doctrine of double effect — and whether palliative sedation falls under it.
Where MAID is legal (Oregon since 1997, Canada since 2016, several others) and where it’s expanding.
Disability community critiques: “better dead than disabled” worries, coercion concerns, and access disparities.
The psychiatric MAID controversy — Belgium, Netherlands, Canada’s pause and ongoing debate.

Visual elements: - Figure (code-generated): concept map distinguishing active/passive, voluntary/involuntary, killing/letting die. - Table: MAID legal status across selected jurisdictions (US states, Canada, Netherlands, Belgium, Switzerland). - Case box: Rachels’ “Smith and Jones in the bathtub” thought experiment. - Discussion prompt: “Is there a moral difference between turning off a ventilator and giving a lethal injection? Why or why not?” - Case box: A recent psychiatric MAID case (with care, given course audience).

title: Course Outline & Learning Outcomes
text:
1.7 L8. Justice & Health Care Allocation

1.7.1 L8a. Theories of Justice and the Right to Health Care

A short tour of four families of theories, in plain language:

Utilitarian — maximize total well-being.
Rawlsian — what would we choose behind a “veil of ignorance”?
Libertarian (Nozick) — no one is owed health care; redistribution is unjust.
Capabilities (Sen, Nussbaum) — what people need to function as humans.

Is there a moral right to health care? Norman Daniels’ argument from fair equality of opportunity.
Where the US stands: a country that treats food, housing, and education as partial rights but health care as mostly a market good.

Visual elements: - Figure (code-generated): 2×2 grid of theories of justice along axes of “individual liberty ↔︎ collective welfare” and “outcome-focused ↔︎ rights-focused.” - Table: Four theories applied to one scenario (e.g., “should an expensive cancer drug be covered by public insurance?”) — what each says and why. - Sidebar: Rawls’ original position, illustrated as a thought experiment. - Discussion prompt: “Behind the veil of ignorance, what health system would you design?”


1.7.2 L8b. Real-World Disparities and Allocation Choices

Comparing systems: US, Canada, Germany, UK on coverage, financing, outcomes (life expectancy, infant mortality, cost per capita).
Health disparities in the US: racial, socioeconomic, geographic — and the mechanisms behind them (access, bias, environment, history).
Implicit bias in clinical encounters — what the evidence says (pain undertreatment, dismissed symptoms), and what interventions show promise.
Organ allocation: how UNOS prioritizes, the ethics of directed donation, “first-come” vs. “sickest first” vs. “best outcome.”
Rationing under scarcity (preview of COVID-era crisis standards, fully developed in L9).

Visual elements: - Figure (code-generated): bar chart comparing US/Canada/Germany/UK on life expectancy, infant mortality, and per-capita health spending (using OECD data). - Figure (code-generated): line chart of US life expectancy by race over recent decades. - Table: Health insurance coverage mechanisms — single-payer, multi-payer with mandate, employer-based, etc., with one country example each. - Case box: The 2009 Oregon Health Plan prioritization list — a real attempt at explicit rationing. - Case box: UNOS organ allocation criteria, with a discussion prompt about directed donation.

title: Course Outline & Learning Outcomes
text:
1.8 L9. Public Health Ethics

1.8.1 L9a. From Patient to Population

How public health ethics differs from clinical ethics: the unit of concern shifts from the individual patient to the population.
Five core public health ethics frameworks (e.g., Childress et al.’s benchmarks: effectiveness, proportionality, necessity, least infringement, public justification).
Classic tensions: individual liberty vs. collective welfare.
A short history of public health: sanitation, vaccination, fluoridation, tobacco control — the wins, and the resistance.
Jacobson v. Massachusetts (1905) and the legal basis for vaccine mandates.

Visual elements: - Figure (code-generated): side-by-side comparison of clinical ethics vs. public health ethics — unit of concern, primary value, key tensions. - Figure (code-generated): line graph showing disease incidence before and after major public health interventions (e.g., measles before/after vaccine). - Image (public domain): John Snow’s cholera map (1854), Wikimedia Commons. - Table: Childress et al.’s five “justificatory conditions” with an example for each. - Sidebar: “What did Jacobson actually decide?” — a short legal note.


1.8.2 L9b. Pandemics, Mandates, and What COVID Revealed

Quarantine and isolation: ethical justifications and their limits.
Vaccine mandates and conscientious exemptions — the religious, philosophical, and medical exemption debate.
Crisis standards of care: how triage shifts under scarcity (ventilators, ICU beds, monoclonals).
What COVID-19 revealed:

Pre-existing inequities (race, occupation, housing density)
Governance failures and information environments
The limits of public trust as a resource

Ethical lessons going forward — and what the field is still arguing about.

Visual elements: - Figure (code-generated): a triage decision tree for ventilator allocation under crisis standards (illustrative, with the major scoring approaches noted). - Figure (code-generated): bar chart of COVID-19 mortality by race/ethnicity and by occupation type (CDC data). - Table: Arguments for and against vaccine mandates — public health, autonomy, religious liberty, equity. - Case box: The Italian ICU triage protocols of March 2020. - Discussion prompt: “What would fair allocation of the first 100,000 doses of a pandemic vaccine look like?”

title: Course Outline & Learning Outcomes
text:
1.9 L10. AI in Medicine

1.9.1 L10a. How Medical AI Works and Where It Goes Wrong

A jargon-free explanation of supervised machine learning: training data, features, labels, prediction.
Two main use cases in medicine:

Diagnostic AI (image classification — radiology, dermatology, pathology)
Predictive AI (risk scores — sepsis, readmission, deterioration)

Sources of algorithmic bias:

Training data not representative of deployment population
Proxy variables (using cost as a proxy for need, etc.)
Deployment drift (model performs worse over time as the world changes)

Two case studies students should know:

The Optum care-management algorithm (Obermeyer et al. 2019) — used cost to predict need, systematically underestimated Black patients’ illness.
The Epic Sepsis Model controversy (Wong et al. 2021) — claimed strong performance, performed poorly in independent validation.


Visual elements: - Figure (code-generated): simplified diagram of how a supervised ML model is trained and deployed. - Figure (code-generated): illustration of the Optum proxy-variable problem — cost ≠ need. - Table: Diagnostic vs. predictive medical AI — examples, what could go wrong, how it’s evaluated. - Case box: The Optum algorithm in detail — what it did, how it was caught, what changed. - Sidebar: “Why does a model trained on Stanford patients underperform at a rural hospital?”


1.9.2 L10b. Consent, Liability, and Living with Black Boxes

Informed consent when AI is in the loop: should patients be told? Does the answer change for opaque vs. explainable models?
Liability when AI gets it wrong: clinician, hospital, vendor, regulator — who’s responsible?
AI mental-health chatbots: what they’re good at, what they’re not, and recent harm reports.
Data privacy in health AI: training-data provenance, model memorization, deidentification limits.
The professional ethics of clinicians who use AI: when to defer, when to override.

Visual elements: - Figure (code-generated): decision flow for “should I tell the patient AI was involved?” with the relevant ethical considerations. - Table: Liability scenarios when AI contributes to harm — who’s plausibly responsible under each framing. - Case box: A reported case involving a mental-health chatbot (chosen with care for course audience). - Discussion prompt: “The AI says the patient has a 90% chance of sepsis; the resident sees no clinical signs. What should happen?” - Sidebar: HIPAA and AI — what the rule does and doesn’t cover.

title: Course Outline & Learning Outcomes
text:
1.10 L11. Gender, Identity & Disability

1.10.1 L11a. Gender-Affirming Care: Adults, Adolescents, and the Evidence Debate

A vocabulary primer: sex, gender identity, gender dysphoria — defined neutrally and clinically.
The current standard pathways for adult gender-affirming care (WPATH Standards of Care 8, basics only).
Pediatric and adolescent care: what’s offered, when, with what assessments and safeguards.
The Cass Review (UK, 2024): what it concluded, what it changed in NHS practice, and the contested reception of its evidentiary standards.
Parental authority, adolescent assent, and clinical gatekeeping — applying ideas from L2 in a contested area.

Visual elements: - Figure (code-generated): timeline of care pathways for adults vs. adolescents (typical, illustrative). - Table: Major positions in the Cass Review debate — supporters’ claims, critics’ claims, the evidentiary points each emphasizes. - Sidebar: “What is ‘gender dysphoria’ in DSM-5-TR?” - Discussion prompt: “Where should the age line be for irreversible interventions, and who should decide?” - Case box: A composite case (e.g., a 15-year-old patient, their parents disagreeing) with a four-principles analysis.


1.10.2 L11b. Disability, Identity, and the Limits of Autonomy

Models of disability: medical model vs. social model vs. interactional views.
Barnes (“mere difference” view) vs. Bognar and others (“bad difference”): is disability bad in itself or only because of how society is arranged?
The disability-rights critique of selective abortion (Asch): what it argues, what it doesn’t argue.
Body integrity identity disorder (BIID) / body integrity dysphoria: when patients seek amputation of healthy limbs — what does respect for autonomy require?
Applying the four principles to cases at the intersection of identity and medicine.

Visual elements: - Figure (code-generated): diagram contrasting medical and social models of disability with a specific example (e.g., wheelchair user + stairs vs. ramp). - Table: “Mere difference” vs. “bad difference” — what each accepts, rejects, and implies for selection and policy. - Case box: The BIID debate — a real reported case, briefly. - Discussion prompt: “If a deaf couple wants a deaf child via embryo selection, what should the clinic do?” - Image (public domain): the disability pride flag or relevant historical photograph (verify public domain status before use).

title: Course Outline & Learning Outcomes
text:
1.11 L12. Animal Ethics

1.11.1 L12a. Moral Status of Animals and the Ethics of Research

Three major views on animal moral status:

Kantian / indirect duties — animals matter only because of how their treatment affects humans.
Singer’s utilitarianism — equal consideration of equal interests; species is morally irrelevant.
Regan’s rights view — “subjects-of-a-life” have inherent value.

The 3 Rs of animal research (Russell & Burch, 1959): replacement, reduction, refinement.
IACUCs (the IRB-equivalent for animal research) — how they work.
Primate research: why it’s used (neuroscience, infectious disease), why it’s contested, current trends (declining NIH-funded chimp research).

Visual elements: - Figure (code-generated): spectrum diagram of moral-status views from Kantian to Regan, with where common positions land. - Table: Singer vs. Regan vs. Kantian view — what each says about eating meat, animal research, pets, wildlife. - Figure (code-generated): bar chart of US animal research use by species (NIH/USDA data). - Case box: The 2015 NIH decision to end chimpanzee research. - Sidebar: “What’s actually in an IACUC protocol?”


1.11.2 L12b. Industrial Animals, Xenotransplantation, and Connecting Threads

The ethics of industrial animal agriculture: welfare concerns, public-health implications (zoonotic risk, antibiotic resistance), environmental dimensions.
Xenotransplantation: recent pig-heart transplant cases (Bennett 2022, Faucette 2023), pig-kidney transplant trials.
Ethical issues unique to xenotransplantation: animal welfare, infection risk to recipients and the public, informed consent under desperation.
Connecting threads: how arguments from earlier chapters apply here.

From abortion (L4): moral status criteria — do they pick out animals too?
From genetics (L6): is creating genetically modified pigs for organs different from creating savior siblings?
From end of life (L7): is the pig’s death in xenotransplantation an instance of killing for benefit — and is it justified differently than human research subject risk?


Visual elements: - Figure (code-generated): diagram of how a pig kidney is genetically modified for human transplantation (CRISPR knockouts, transgene insertions — at a high level). - Table: Major xenotransplantation cases since 2022 — recipient, organ, outcome, ethical issues raised. - Figure (code-generated): concept map showing how the moral-status arguments from L4, L6, L7, and L11 each apply (or don’t) to animals. - Case box: The David Bennett pig-heart transplant (2022) — the patient, the team, the outcome, the lessons. - Discussion prompt: “If you accept Marquis’s ‘future like ours’ argument against abortion, what does it say about killing a pig?”

title: Course Outline & Learning Outcomes
text:
1.12 Cross-chapter design notes

Image sourcing. All “public domain” labels should be verified before publication — Wikimedia Commons, NIH/NHGRI, CDC, US National Archives, and the Wellcome Collection (CC0 portion) are the safest sources. When uncertain, fall back to a code-generated figure.
Code-generated figures. All figures marked “code-generated” can be produced in matplotlib or with a TikZ Beamer-style approach if you want a consistent look across this and your LaTeX materials.
Case boxes and discussion prompts. Each subchapter has at least one of each. These are the high-leverage assessment points and also map well to your CSV quiz workflow.
Tying it together. L12b deliberately revisits L4, L6, L7, and L11 to make the course feel cumulative rather than modular — feel free to flag similar callbacks in L7b (→ L4), L11b (→ L5/L6), and L10b (→ L2).

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
section: A Quiet Tuesday on the Floor
text:
A Quiet Tuesday on the Floor

A 34-year-old woman is in the trauma bay after a high-speed crash. Alert, oriented, full capacity. CT shows a grade IV splenic laceration; her hemoglobin has fallen from 13 to 7 in the last hour. The surgeon wants her in the OR, with pre-emptive transfusion. Tucked in her wallet is a signed, dated card declaring her a Jehovah’s Witness and refusing all blood products. Her parents have just arrived and are pleading with the team to “do whatever it takes.”
You are the nurse at her bedside.

What questions would you want answered before doing anything?
Who exactly is asking you to do what?
What do you think the right move is — and why?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


The legal frame. U.S. courts have, in nearly every case involving a competent adult, upheld the patient’s right to refuse blood — even when refusal is fatal. The harder cases involve minors, pregnancy, and unclear capacity. We will come back to all three later in the course.




Let students sit with this. We’ll come back to it after we have vocabulary. The whole rest of the lecture is, in a sense, equipping us to answer this question more carefully than “follow your gut.”

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Today

By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

Define bioethics and explain why it emerged as a distinct field
Distinguish ethical claims from legal, religious, and personal-preference claims
Name and apply the four principles of biomedical ethics
Identify the premises and conclusion of a moral argument written in ordinary prose
Recognize a few common fallacies in bioethical reasoning

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
A Working Definition
Bioethics is the systematic study of ethical questions arising in:

The care of patients (clinical ethics)
Biomedical research on humans and animals (research ethics)
Public health policy and the allocation of scarce resources
New biotechnologies — genetics, AI, reproductive technology


“Systematic” matters. Bioethics isn’t just having opinions — it’s reasoning about them in a way others can examine, challenge, and refine.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Why a Distinct Field?
A short list of mid-20th-century shocks:

The Nazi medical experiments exposed at Nuremberg (1945–1947)
The Tuskegee syphilis study (1932–1972), in which Black men were left untreated for decades
New technology that outran old norms: ventilators, dialysis, organ transplants, IVF
Patients and families demanding a voice the old paternalistic model didn’t give them

Bioethics is what grew up to answer the question: how should we govern this?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Three Sample Questions

Clinical: Can a 16-year-old refuse chemotherapy her parents want her to have?
Research: Is it ethical to give half the participants a placebo when an effective drug already exists?
Policy: When ICU beds run out in a pandemic, who gets one?


Each of these is a recurring exam case. Same field, very different scales — bedside, study, society.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Why the Distinctions Matter
Students often collapse these. They are not the same — and confusing them is one of the most common mistakes in bioethical reasoning.








Type of claim
Source of authority
Question it answers




Ethical
Reasons we can examine and share
What ought we to do?


Legal
Statutes, courts, regulators
What may or must we do under law?


Religious
Sacred texts, traditions, communities
What does this faith require?


Preference
Personal taste
What do I happen to want?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Ethics vs. Law
The law is not a reliable guide to what is ethical.

Slavery in the United States was legal until 1865 — and was monstrously unethical the entire time.
The Tuskegee study was legal in 1932. It was wrong in 1932.
Conversely, jaywalking is illegal in most cities; almost no one thinks it is a moral failing.







Tip


A useful test for nurses: “Is it legal?” and “Is it the right thing for this patient?” are two different questions. Sometimes the law lags behind ethics; sometimes it overreaches.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Ethics vs. Religion
Many of our patients — and many of you — draw moral guidance from religious tradition. That is appropriate. But:

A religious tradition can give its members reasons to act, but cannot, on its own, give non-members reasons to act.
Bioethical arguments need to work in a pluralistic society where patients, families, and clinicians come from many traditions and none.

We will treat religious commitments as important data about what patients value — not as conversation-stoppers in clinical reasoning.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Ethics vs. Personal Preference

“I just don’t think it’s right.”

That is a feeling, not yet an argument. Ethical reasoning asks the next question: why? What reason, that another reasonable person could weigh, supports the conclusion?

This is the moment to flag that “I feel” / “I believe” are perfectly fine starting points — but bioethics asks us to take the next step.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Thought Question

A nurse on the L&D floor is asked to assist with a second-trimester abortion for a patient whose fetus has a lethal anomaly. The procedure is legal; her own physician is performing it. The nurse declines on religious grounds and asks to be reassigned. Her hospital has a conscience clause — a policy permitting clinicians to opt out of specific procedures — and she has invoked it formally.

Is what she is doing legal? Ethical? Religiously required? A personal preference?
Could the answers come apart? In what scenario?
Now imagine the same refusal at 3 a.m. at a small rural hospital with no other nurse on shift. Anything change?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


Where the duty actually lands. Most U.S. conscience-clause laws place the duty to still care for the patient on the institution, not on the individual clinician. That shifts the question from “can she refuse?” to “what does the institution owe the patient when she does?”

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
A Brief Origin Story
In the wake of Tuskegee, a U.S. national commission produced the Belmont Report (1979) (National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research 1979). That same year, philosophers Tom Beauchamp and James Childress published Principles of Biomedical Ethics (Beauchamp and Childress 2019), proposing four principles meant to be:

Shared across ethical traditions
Specific enough to guide clinical decisions
General enough to apply to new technologies

The result is what’s often called principlism — and it remains the lingua franca of hospital ethics consults (Jonsen 1998).

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
1. Autonomy
Autonomy — self-rule. Respect the patient’s right to make informed decisions about her own body and care.
In practice, autonomy underwrites:

Informed consent
The right to refuse treatment (even life-saving treatment)
Truth-telling and honest disclosure
Confidentiality


The Jehovah’s Witness case from the start of class is, at its core, an autonomy case. Hold that thought.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Autonomy in Practice

Autonomy self-rule


The case. A 28-year-old with type-1 diabetes (A1c 11.2) declines an insulin pump for the third time. She is tired of being told what to do.
What respecting autonomy looks like here:

Make sure she understands the risks — disclosure
Assess decision-making capacity (she has it)
Document clearly; continue to offer support without coercion

Autonomy is the floor. It does not require us to abandon her — only not to override her.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
2. Beneficence
Beneficence — do good. Act in the patient’s best interest; promote welfare.
This sounds obvious until you ask: whose account of the patient’s good?

The clinician’s professional judgment?
The patient’s own values?
The family’s hopes?

Beneficence and autonomy can pull in opposite directions.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Beneficence in Practice

Beneficence do good


The case. An 82-year-old with end-stage heart failure, fifth admission this year. The team recommends comfort-focused care; he wants “everything done.” His daughter asks the team to “talk him out of it.”
Whose idea of his good?

The team’s clinical judgment about prognosis?
His own stated wishes, formed over a long life?
His daughter’s fear of losing him?

Beneficence is not the same as paternalism. It is the discipline of finding out what this person counts as a life worth living — and acting in service of that.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3. Non-Maleficence
Non-maleficence — first, do no harm (primum non nocere).


The oldest principle in Western medicine, traceable to the Hippocratic tradition (5th c. BCE). It does not mean “never do anything risky.” It means: weigh the harms, and don’t expose patients to harm without proportionate benefit.
Every chemotherapy regimen, every surgery, every medication is a deliberate small harm in service of a larger good.



Hippocrates, engraving by Peter Paul Rubens. Public domain (Wikimedia Commons).

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Non-Maleficence in Practice

Non-Maleficence do no harm


The case. A new chemotherapy regimen offers a 30% chance of remission and a 5% chance of fatal cardiotoxicity. Standard care offers neither.
The principle does not say “never harm.” It asks:

Is the expected harm proportionate to the expected benefit?
Does the patient understand both sides clearly enough to choose?

Every IV start is a small puncture wound. Every medication has side effects. The principle’s job is to forbid hidden, gratuitous, or disproportionate risk — not all risk.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4. Justice
Justice — fair distribution of benefits and burdens.
In bioethics, justice shows up in three main forms:

Distributive justice — who gets the kidney, the ICU bed, the new drug?
Procedural justice — was the process fair, even if the outcome is hard?
Social justice — are we honest about the historical and structural causes of health disparities?


We’ll spend an entire lecture (L8) on justice. For now, just plant the flag: bioethics is not only about individual encounters.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Justice in Practice

Justice treat fairly


The case. Two 55-year-olds arrive in the ED with chest pain and no obvious red flags. One is on Medicaid; the other has private insurance. Decades of research show that, on average, they will not get the same workup.
Justice at the bedside is rarely a kidney allocation decision. It looks like:

Are we ordering tests for clinical reasons — or for who the patient appears to be?
Are our floors and ICUs staffed and supplied equitably?
Where do we, as nurses, push back against disparate care?

Justice asks us to look up from the individual patient and notice the pattern.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
The Four Together





flowchart LR
  A[Autonomy<br/>respect choices] --- B[Beneficence<br/>do good]
  B --- N[Non-maleficence<br/>do no harm]
  N --- J[Justice<br/>treat fairly]
  J --- A
  style A fill:#cfe9ec,stroke:#0e7c86,stroke-width:2px
  style B fill:#fce9d6,stroke:#b07d1a,stroke-width:2px
  style N fill:#fad7d2,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px
  style J fill:#dfe6ee,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px






A framework, not an algorithm. The principles tell you what to weigh — not, by themselves, how to weigh it.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
When Principles Conflict
Most interesting bioethics cases are conflicts between principles, not conflicts between good and evil.

Autonomy vs. Beneficence — the Jehovah’s Witness refusing transfusion
Beneficence vs. Non-Maleficence — risky surgery with a small chance of cure
Autonomy vs. Justice — vaccine refusal during a pandemic
Beneficence vs. Justice — spending heroic resources on one patient when others wait

The four principles do not resolve these tensions. They make them visible so we can argue about them carefully.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Thought Question

Return to our trauma-bay patient — full capacity, signed refusal, parents at the door. Now you have vocabulary for what was hard.

Which principles point toward transfusing her? Which point away?
Which counts as the “harm” — letting her die, or doing something to her body she has explicitly refused?
Which principle would you weight most heavily, and why?
What single fact, if changed, would change your answer?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


How the principles typically map.

The case is often framed as Beneficence and Non-Maleficence pulling toward transfusion, Autonomy pulling against.
But notice the twist: respecting her refusal may itself be the most beneficent act, and transfusing a competent patient against her will is itself a form of harm.
The principles do not decide the case. They give us shared language to argue about it.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
What Is an Argument?
An argument, in philosophy, is not a quarrel. It is a structured piece of reasoning:

One or more premises (claims offered as support)
A conclusion (the claim the premises are meant to support)

The job of the careful reader: find the conclusion first, then the premises, then ask whether the premises are true and whether they actually support the conclusion.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Worked Example
A short argument you might hear on the floor:

“We have to transfuse her — she’ll die otherwise, and our job is to save lives.”








argument


P1

Premise 1
Without a transfusion,
the patient will die.



C

∴  Conclusion
We should transfuse her.



P1->C





P2

Premise 2
Our job is
to save lives.



P2->C

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Hidden Assumptions
Sounds airtight — until you notice what’s missing. That argument quietly assumes:

“Saving lives” always outweighs the patient’s other wishes.
The patient’s refusal can be set aside because it’s “irrational.”
A nurse’s professional duty is identical to “extending biological life.”

Each of those is contestable. Surfacing hidden assumptions is half the work of bioethics.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
What Is a Fallacy?
A fallacy is an error in reasoning — an argument that looks like good support but isn’t.

Not the same as a lie. The speaker may sincerely believe it.
A fallacious argument can still have a true conclusion — the defect is in the structure, not the conclusion.
Most bioethics fallacies are informal: the form looks fine, but the content misleads through irrelevance, ambiguity, or hidden assumption.

The next two slides cover the six you will see most often.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Common Fallacies in Bioethics (1/2)


Appeal to Nature Inferring that something is good (or bad) merely because it is natural (or unnatural). “IVF lets older women conceive — but it’s unnatural, so it must be wrong.”


Slippery Slope Predicting that allowing X will inevitably lead to a much worse Y, without showing how the slide actually happens. “If we permit medical aid in dying for the terminally ill, we’ll end up euthanizing the disabled.”


Ad Hominem Attacking the person making the argument rather than the argument itself. “You can’t take Peter Singer seriously on animal ethics — he holds a lot of unpopular views.”

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Common Fallacies in Bioethics (2/2)


Naturalistic Fallacy Inferring an ought directly from an is — that what happens in nature shows what should happen morally. “Humans evolved as omnivores, therefore eating meat is morally permissible.”


False Dichotomy Framing a complex issue as if there were only two options, when others exist. “Either we let the market ration health care, or the system will collapse.”


Appeal to Authority Treating the testimony of an authority as decisive even when they speak outside their expertise — or when other experts disagree. “My favorite podcaster says vaccines cause autism, and he has done a lot of research.”

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Practice — Spot the Fallacy

Identify the fallacy in each:

“Gene editing is wrong — it’s playing God.”
“If we let doctors help patients die, we’ll end up euthanizing the disabled.”
“You can’t trust her opinion on vaccines — she’s not even a real scientist.”
“Either we ration care by ability to pay, or the system collapses.”



Answers (in order): appeal to nature, slippery slope, ad hominem, false dichotomy.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
Wrapping Up Part A

Bioethics is the systematic study of moral questions in medicine, research, and policy. It is not the same as law, religion, or personal preference. The four principles — autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, justice — give us a shared vocabulary for the conflicts we’ll see all semester. And every claim in this course will be evaluated as an argument: premises, conclusion, hidden assumptions, possible fallacies.
In Part B, we ask the next question: where do these principles come from? We meet the major ethical theories — utilitarian, Kantian, virtue, and care — that ground and sometimes contest the four principles.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
A 34-year-old woman is in the trauma bay after a high-speed crash. Alert, oriented, full capacity. CT shows a grade IV splenic laceration; her hemoglobin has fallen from 13 to 7 in the last hour. The surgeon wants her in the OR, with pre-emptive transfusion. Tucked in her wallet is a signed, dated card declaring her a Jehovah’s Witness and refusing all blood products. Her parents have just arrived and are pleading with the team to “do whatever it takes.”
You are the nurse at her bedside.

What questions would you want answered before doing anything?
Who exactly is asking you to do what?
What do you think the right move is — and why?











Note



The legal frame. U.S. courts have, in nearly every case involving a competent adult, upheld the patient’s right to refuse blood — even when refusal is fatal. The harder cases involve minors, pregnancy, and unclear capacity. We will come back to all three later in the course.



Let students sit with this. We’ll come back to it after we have vocabulary. The whole rest of the lecture is, in a sense, equipping us to answer this question more carefully than “follow your gut.”





By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

Define bioethics and explain why it emerged as a distinct field
Distinguish ethical claims from legal, religious, and personal-preference claims
Name and apply the four principles of biomedical ethics
Identify the premises and conclusion of a moral argument written in ordinary prose
Recognize a few common fallacies in bioethical reasoning

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
2.1 A Working Definition
Bioethics is the systematic study of ethical questions arising in:

The care of patients (clinical ethics)
Biomedical research on humans and animals (research ethics)
Public health policy and the allocation of scarce resources
New biotechnologies — genetics, AI, reproductive technology


“Systematic” matters. Bioethics isn’t just having opinions — it’s reasoning about them in a way others can examine, challenge, and refine.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
2.2 Why a Distinct Field?
A short list of mid-20th-century shocks:

The Nazi medical experiments exposed at Nuremberg (1945–1947)
The Tuskegee syphilis study (1932–1972), in which Black men were left untreated for decades
New technology that outran old norms: ventilators, dialysis, organ transplants, IVF
Patients and families demanding a voice the old paternalistic model didn’t give them

Bioethics is what grew up to answer the question: how should we govern this?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
2.3 Three Sample Questions


Clinical: Can a 16-year-old refuse chemotherapy her parents want her to have?
Research: Is it ethical to give half the participants a placebo when an effective drug already exists?
Policy: When ICU beds run out in a pandemic, who gets one?



Each of these is a recurring exam case. Same field, very different scales — bedside, study, society.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.1 Why the Distinctions Matter
Students often collapse these. They are not the same — and confusing them is one of the most common mistakes in bioethical reasoning.








Type of claim
Source of authority
Question it answers




Ethical
Reasons we can examine and share
What ought we to do?


Legal
Statutes, courts, regulators
What may or must we do under law?


Religious
Sacred texts, traditions, communities
What does this faith require?


Preference
Personal taste
What do I happen to want?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.2 Ethics vs. Law
The law is not a reliable guide to what is ethical.

Slavery in the United States was legal until 1865 — and was monstrously unethical the entire time.
The Tuskegee study was legal in 1932. It was wrong in 1932.
Conversely, jaywalking is illegal in most cities; almost no one thinks it is a moral failing.







Tip



A useful test for nurses: “Is it legal?” and “Is it the right thing for this patient?” are two different questions. Sometimes the law lags behind ethics; sometimes it overreaches.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.3 Ethics vs. Religion
Many of our patients — and many of you — draw moral guidance from religious tradition. That is appropriate. But:

A religious tradition can give its members reasons to act, but cannot, on its own, give non-members reasons to act.
Bioethical arguments need to work in a pluralistic society where patients, families, and clinicians come from many traditions and none.

We will treat religious commitments as important data about what patients value — not as conversation-stoppers in clinical reasoning.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.4 Ethics vs. Personal Preference

“I just don’t think it’s right.”

That is a feeling, not yet an argument. Ethical reasoning asks the next question: why? What reason, that another reasonable person could weigh, supports the conclusion?

This is the moment to flag that “I feel” / “I believe” are perfectly fine starting points — but bioethics asks us to take the next step.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.5 Thought Question

A nurse on the L&D floor is asked to assist with a second-trimester abortion for a patient whose fetus has a lethal anomaly. The procedure is legal; her own physician is performing it. The nurse declines on religious grounds and asks to be reassigned. Her hospital has a conscience clause — a policy permitting clinicians to opt out of specific procedures — and she has invoked it formally.

Is what she is doing legal? Ethical? Religiously required? A personal preference?
Could the answers come apart? In what scenario?
Now imagine the same refusal at 3 a.m. at a small rural hospital with no other nurse on shift. Anything change?

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
3.6 After You’ve Discussed






Note



Where the duty actually lands. Most U.S. conscience-clause laws place the duty to still care for the patient on the institution, not on the individual clinician. That shifts the question from “can she refuse?” to “what does the institution owe the patient when she does?”

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.1 A Brief Origin Story
In the wake of Tuskegee, a U.S. national commission produced the Belmont Report (1979) (National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research 1979). That same year, philosophers Tom Beauchamp and James Childress published Principles of Biomedical Ethics (Beauchamp and Childress 2019), proposing four principles meant to be:

Shared across ethical traditions
Specific enough to guide clinical decisions
General enough to apply to new technologies

The result is what’s often called principlism — and it remains the lingua franca of hospital ethics consults (Jonsen 1998).

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.2 1. Autonomy
Autonomy — self-rule. Respect the patient’s right to make informed decisions about her own body and care.
In practice, autonomy underwrites:

Informed consent
The right to refuse treatment (even life-saving treatment)
Truth-telling and honest disclosure
Confidentiality


The Jehovah’s Witness case from the start of class is, at its core, an autonomy case. Hold that thought.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.3 Autonomy in Practice

Autonomy self-rule


The case. A 28-year-old with type-1 diabetes (A1c 11.2) declines an insulin pump for the third time. She is tired of being told what to do.
What respecting autonomy looks like here:

Make sure she understands the risks — disclosure
Assess decision-making capacity (she has it)
Document clearly; continue to offer support without coercion

Autonomy is the floor. It does not require us to abandon her — only not to override her.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.4 2. Beneficence
Beneficence — do good. Act in the patient’s best interest; promote welfare.
This sounds obvious until you ask: whose account of the patient’s good?

The clinician’s professional judgment?
The patient’s own values?
The family’s hopes?

Beneficence and autonomy can pull in opposite directions.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.5 Beneficence in Practice

Beneficence do good


The case. An 82-year-old with end-stage heart failure, fifth admission this year. The team recommends comfort-focused care; he wants “everything done.” His daughter asks the team to “talk him out of it.”
Whose idea of his good?

The team’s clinical judgment about prognosis?
His own stated wishes, formed over a long life?
His daughter’s fear of losing him?

Beneficence is not the same as paternalism. It is the discipline of finding out what this person counts as a life worth living — and acting in service of that.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.6 3. Non-Maleficence
Non-maleficence — first, do no harm (primum non nocere).


The oldest principle in Western medicine, traceable to the Hippocratic tradition (5th c. BCE). It does not mean “never do anything risky.” It means: weigh the harms, and don’t expose patients to harm without proportionate benefit.
Every chemotherapy regimen, every surgery, every medication is a deliberate small harm in service of a larger good.



Hippocrates, engraving by Peter Paul Rubens. Public domain (Wikimedia Commons).

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.7 Non-Maleficence in Practice

Non-Maleficence do no harm


The case. A new chemotherapy regimen offers a 30% chance of remission and a 5% chance of fatal cardiotoxicity. Standard care offers neither.
The principle does not say “never harm.” It asks:

Is the expected harm proportionate to the expected benefit?
Does the patient understand both sides clearly enough to choose?

Every IV start is a small puncture wound. Every medication has side effects. The principle’s job is to forbid hidden, gratuitous, or disproportionate risk — not all risk.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.8 4. Justice
Justice — fair distribution of benefits and burdens.
In bioethics, justice shows up in three main forms:

Distributive justice — who gets the kidney, the ICU bed, the new drug?
Procedural justice — was the process fair, even if the outcome is hard?
Social justice — are we honest about the historical and structural causes of health disparities?


We’ll spend an entire lecture (L8) on justice. For now, just plant the flag: bioethics is not only about individual encounters.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.9 Justice in Practice

Justice treat fairly


The case. Two 55-year-olds arrive in the ED with chest pain and no obvious red flags. One is on Medicaid; the other has private insurance. Decades of research show that, on average, they will not get the same workup.
Justice at the bedside is rarely a kidney allocation decision. It looks like:

Are we ordering tests for clinical reasons — or for who the patient appears to be?
Are our floors and ICUs staffed and supplied equitably?
Where do we, as nurses, push back against disparate care?

Justice asks us to look up from the individual patient and notice the pattern.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.10 The Four Together





flowchart LR
  A[Autonomy<br/>respect choices] --- B[Beneficence<br/>do good]
  B --- N[Non-maleficence<br/>do no harm]
  N --- J[Justice<br/>treat fairly]
  J --- A
  style A fill:#cfe9ec,stroke:#0e7c86,stroke-width:2px
  style B fill:#fce9d6,stroke:#b07d1a,stroke-width:2px
  style N fill:#fad7d2,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px
  style J fill:#dfe6ee,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px






A framework, not an algorithm. The principles tell you what to weigh — not, by themselves, how to weigh it.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.11 When Principles Conflict
Most interesting bioethics cases are conflicts between principles, not conflicts between good and evil.

Autonomy vs. Beneficence — the Jehovah’s Witness refusing transfusion
Beneficence vs. Non-Maleficence — risky surgery with a small chance of cure
Autonomy vs. Justice — vaccine refusal during a pandemic
Beneficence vs. Justice — spending heroic resources on one patient when others wait

The four principles do not resolve these tensions. They make them visible so we can argue about them carefully.

title: Principles, Theories & Moral Reasoning
text:
4.12 Thought Question

Return to our trauma-bay patient — full capacity, signed refusal, parents at the door. Now you have vocabulary for what was hard.

Which principles point toward transfusing her? Which point away?
Which counts as the “harm” — letting her die, or doing something to her body she has explicitly refused?
Which principle would you weight most heavily, and why?
What single fact, if changed, would change your answer?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
A Study You Should Refuse

You are a physician asked to help run a syphilis study.

The subjects are poor Black men in Alabama.
They are told they have “bad blood,” not syphilis.
Penicillin now works, but the study keeps going.
Your supervisors say the knowledge will help future patients.

Do you stay in the study? If not, what principle tells you to walk away?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


Hold onto your answer. Several of the reasons you gave for walking away were not obvious to physicians at the time. They had to be learned case by case, and eventually written down. Part A traces how that happened.




Start with moral discomfort before giving students the vocabulary. The point is to make the historical material feel like an answer to a real problem they already feel.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Today

By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

Trace the move from “doctor knows best” to patient consent
Tell what actually happened in the Carrie Buck, Nuremberg, Willowbrook, and Tuskegee cases
Explain which protection each scandal forced into existence
Compare the Nuremberg Code, Helsinki, and the Belmont Report
Explain why IRBs and ethics committees exist today

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
The Deal in One Sentence



The doctor decides. The patient trusts. No one else asks.
Western medicine’s oldest model, traced to the Hippocratic tradition
Built on real duties: help the sick, “do no harm,” keep confidences
One name for it: paternalism — caring for you by deciding for you




Hippocrates of Kos. Public domain. (U.S. National Library of Medicine 2002)

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: Its Bright Side and Its Dark Side
text:
Its Bright Side and Its Dark Side


The bright side

Clinicians really do know more
Emergencies need fast judgment
Patients often want guidance, not a menu
Medicine cannot work without professional judgment


The dark side

Expertise slides into domination
Good intentions can hide coercion
The patient becomes an object of care
The profession can protect itself first

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
A Bedside Moment

Protective lying
A physician tells a family, “Don’t tell him it is cancer yet. He will give up.”

Kindness or control?
Beneficence or disrespect?
What is missing from this encounter?



Paternalism was usually defended as expertise plus benevolence, not cruelty. That is exactly why it lasted so long — and why the abuses ahead were so hard to see from the inside.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
The Idea Called Eugenics

Eugenics: the belief that society can be improved by controlling who is allowed to have children
“Good” traits encouraged; “bad” traits — disability, poverty, the “wrong” ancestry — bred out
It was not fringe: it was backed by respected scientists
In the United States, it was backed by law

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Carrie Buck, Age 17

Virginia, 1924. Carrie is poor and a foster child.
She becomes pregnant after being raped by a relative of her foster family.
To hide it, she is labeled “feebleminded” and committed to a state institution.
Her infant daughter, Vivian, is judged “defective” after a few minutes’ observation.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
“Three Generations” — Buck v. Bell (1927)

Virginia wants Carrie sterilized so she can have no more children
The U.S. Supreme Court agrees, 8 to 1 (Supreme Court of the United States 1927)
Justice Holmes writes: “Three generations of imbeciles are enough.”
Carrie is sterilized at 17. Vivian later makes her school’s honor roll.
The law did not stop the harm — the law ordered it

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
The Scoreboard So Far








Scandal
What was missing
The rule we got




Eugenics & Carrie Buck (1927)
Your body is yours; the state cannot breed people
Nothing yet — the courts called this legal




The scoreboard is our running spine. Each disaster leaves a gap; later, a code fills it. Right now the gap is wide open.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Thought Question

You are the physician at the Virginia institution in 1927. The court order to sterilize Carrie has arrived.

Your supervisor calls it legal, routine, and “kind to society.”
You suspect Carrie’s “feeblemindedness” is really poverty and a pregnancy from rape.


Do you perform the operation? If you refuse, what are you appealing to — the law says you are the one in the wrong.
Would it change anything if Carrie herself said yes? Could she give real consent in this situation?
The state’s reasoning is “fewer ‘unfit’ children means a better society.” What is wrong with that argument even when it is sincere?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Make It Contemporary

This is not safely in the past:

2006–2010: about 150 women in California prisons were sterilized, many under pressure and without proper approval.
2017: a Tennessee judge offered reduced jail time to inmates who agreed to a vasectomy or a long-acting contraceptive.

Same question: where is the line between an offer, pressure, and coercion — and who is too constrained to truly say yes?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


Three things to draw out:

“Legal” did not mean “right.” Buck v. Bell has never been formally overturned — law can lag ethics by decades.
Consent does not erase coercion. A “yes” from a confined, dependent person may not be free. Ask who is asking and what saying no would cost.
“For the good of society” is the move to watch. Trading one person’s body for a population-level gain treats her as a means — exactly the reasoning the later codes were written to block.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Medicine Inside the Camps

In Nazi concentration camps, prisoners were used as research material
Refusal was not an option a prisoner had
One example: prisoners were immersed in ice water for hours to time how long downed pilots might survive
Many were injured, disabled, or killed — “science” was the excuse

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
The Doctors’ Trial (1946–1947)

23 defendants from the Nazi medical system
Charges: war crimes and crimes against humanity
The judges had outrage but no written standard to point to
So they wrote one

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: Nuremberg Code: One Line Changes Everything
text:
Nuremberg Code: One Line Changes Everything


“The voluntary consent of the human subject is absolutely essential.”


The Nuremberg Code (Nuremberg Military Tribunals 1947)



Voluntary consent is now non-negotiable
Science never outranks the person in front of you

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
The Code’s Other Plain Rules

The study must have a real purpose, not just curiosity
Risks must be cut to the minimum, and be worth it
The subject can quit at any time, for any reason
The researcher must stop if it turns dangerous

text:
The Scoreboard So Far








Scandal
What was missing
The rule we got

(gap still open)


Nazi experiments → Nuremberg Code (1947)
The subject’s free “yes”

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
section: Why One Code Was Not Enough
text:
Why One Code Was Not Enough

Nuremberg came from judges, about Nazis — many U.S. doctors thought “not us”
The World Medical Association wrote the Declaration of Helsinki (1964) to make research ethics medicine’s own job (World Medical Association 1964)
But rules on paper do not help if no one outside the lab ever checks
Two American cases proved it

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Willowbrook



Willowbrook was a vastly overcrowded New York institution for children with intellectual disabilities
Hepatitis spread constantly through its wards
Researchers deliberately gave the virus to children to study it and test a treatment
The defense offered: “they would have caught it here anyway”




Willowbrook State School administration building postcard, via Wikimedia Commons and the New York Public Library, public domain.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Was the Consent Real?

The institution was full
For a time, the one open door was the research ward
So parents were told, in effect: enroll your child, or wait years for a bed
A signature was collected. Was it a free choice?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Beecher’s Warning (1966)

Henry Beecher was a respected Harvard anesthesiologist — an insider, not a critic
In a leading medical journal he laid out 22 unethical studies already published in respected journals (Beecher 1966)
Examples: penicillin withheld from soldiers to study rheumatic fever; live cancer cells injected into chronically ill patients without their knowledge; the Willowbrook hepatitis studies themselves
These were funded, published, and praised — done by ordinary, successful researchers

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
What Beecher Actually Argued

His sharpest point: a signed form is not the real safeguard — consent can be an empty ritual
He noted the journals were complicit by publishing the work at all
He put more trust in a conscientious investigator than in paperwork
History drew a different lesson: character is not enough — review has to be structural (the coming IRB)


The tension is the teaching point: Beecher bet on individual virtue; the system that followed bet on structure. A good aside: do we actually need both, and does either work without the other?

text:
The Scoreboard So Far








Scandal
What was missing
The rule we got




Eugenics & Carrie Buck (1927)
Your body is yours
(gap still open)


Nazi experiments → Nuremberg (1947)
The subject’s free “yes”
Voluntary consent


Willowbrook & Beecher (1956–66)
Someone outside the team must say yes
Independent review — the future IRB

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Thought Question

Your child has a severe intellectual disability and you cannot care for him at home.

The only institution with an open bed will admit him — but only into the hepatitis research ward.
The wait for a non-research bed is years.


You sign the form. Was your consent voluntary? What would have to be true for it to count?
Researchers said the children “would have caught hepatitis here anyway.” Is that a good reason to deliberately give it to them?
Suppose instead the research ward simply had better staffing and nicer conditions. Is that offer fine, or still a problem?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Make It Contemporary

The same shape recurs today:

A patient enrolls in a drug trial because it is the only way to afford a medication they need.
A Phase I study offers $5,000 to healthy volunteers; the people who sign up are mostly those who most need the money.

No one lied; everyone signed. Is the trial still taking advantage of these people?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


Three different failures hide in this one case — keep them apart:

Coercion — a threat (“no bed unless you enroll”). The choice is not free.
Undue inducement — an offer so strong it clouds judgment ($5,000; the better ward).
Exploitation — taking unfair advantage of someone’s bad situation, even with honest consent and no deception.

And the “they’d get it anyway” defense fails: a background risk does not license deliberately imposing harm (Jonsen 1998).

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Tuskegee: The Setup



Began in 1932 under the U.S. Public Health Service (Brandt 1978)
600 Black men were enrolled in Alabama
399 had syphilis
Researchers wanted to observe the untreated disease
The men were poor and medically underserved




CDC Archives. Public domain.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Tuskegee: The Deception

Men were not told they had syphilis
They were told they had “bad blood”
Free meals, exams, and burial insurance encouraged participation
The study presented itself as care
In reality, observation mattered more than the patients’ welfare

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Tuskegee: Penicillin Withheld

Penicillin became standard therapy in the 1940s
Researchers still did not treat the men
They even interfered with outside access to care
The study continued toward autopsy, not cure
The subjects were being used as means, not ends

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Tuskegee: Why It Lasted

Subjects were poor and politically weak
Racism made their suffering easier to ignore
The study was bureaucratically routine
Professional deference discouraged whistleblowing
There was no external review strong enough to stop it

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Tuskegee: Public Reaction

Exposed by the press in 1972
Public outrage was immediate
Trust in medical institutions was deeply damaged
The damage was especially severe in Black communities
The U.S. could no longer rely on professional self-policing alone

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Thought Question

What was the deepest wrong in Tuskegee?

The deception?
The withholding of treatment?
The racist choice of subjects?
The 40 years of institutional indifference?

You can argue for more than one answer, but which wrong seems most central?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Make It Contemporary


In 1997 the U.S. government formally apologized for Tuskegee — 25 years after it ended.
The justice worry has partly flipped: women and minority groups have often been left out of trials, so treatments are less well tested on them.

Is excluding a group from research a justice failure of the same family as Tuskegee, or a different kind of wrong?

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
After You’ve Discussed






Note


Two payoffs worth the time:

Map each wrong to a principle. Deception → respect for persons; withheld penicillin → beneficence; choosing poor Black men → justice. The 40 years of silence maps to none of them cleanly — codes do not legislate moral courage.
Justice runs both ways. Tuskegee was wrongful inclusion under exploitation. Modern ethics also worries about wrongful exclusion — fair selection means neither dumping risk on the vulnerable nor denying them benefit.

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
The Word “Bioethics”

Van Rensselaer Potter popularized the term bioethics in 1970 (Potter 1970)
He imagined a bridge between science and human values
The field grew quickly in the 1970s
New centers formed at Hastings and Georgetown
Medicine needed public moral language, not just internal rules

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
National Research Act (1974)

Congress responded directly to the research ethics crisis
The Act created a national commission
The commission studied how to protect human subjects
The goal was not only punishment
The goal was a durable ethical framework

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Belmont Report (1979)

The Belmont Report was published in 1979 (National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research 1979)
Short, influential, and still central
Not a checklist
Not a criminal code
A framework for thinking about research ethics

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Respect for Persons

Respect for Persons starts from the idea that individuals are agents
Consent matters because persons are not tools
Those with diminished autonomy need added protection
This principle anchors informed consent
It carries the strongest anti-paternalist force

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Beneficence

Beneficence means more than avoiding catastrophe
Also try to secure subjects’ welfare
Minimize likely harms
Maximize likely benefits
Make risk-benefit judgment explicit rather than casual

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Justice

Justice asks who bears the burdens
Ask who receives the benefits
Ask whether easy-to-exploit groups are being targeted
Tuskegee is a classic failure of justice
Fair subject selection becomes an ethical requirement

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
Three Foundational Documents Compared









Document
Origin
Main emphasis
Best one-line summary




Nuremberg Code
War-crimes tribunal
Voluntary consent
Persons are not raw material


Helsinki
Medical profession
Research governance by physicians
Research needs disciplined review


Belmont
U.S. national commission
Respect, beneficence, justice
Turn principles into oversight

title: Lecture 2 — History & the Clinical Relationship
text:
What IRBs Do

IRBs review proposed human-subject research
Ask whether risks are minimized
Ask whether risks are justified
Ask whether subjects are selected fairly
Ask whether consent protections are adequate
